requested it, had visibility on what the

were in front of it. We could review how

was there already an existing asset that
was that was done. Does one person have

team. We have these many in the queue.
would you like to rep prioritize any of

## 8. A guide to new and vintage cameras for creatives

This is a creative director's guide to
cameras. Now, I'm not a camera reviewer.
This isn't your average camera channel.
We're not going to teach you how to be
an amazing photographer. But if your job
is an art director, a creative director,
a strategist, and you want to make
standout content on social media, or for
your personal brand, I'm going to give
you all the tricks I've accumulated
through 15 plus years of this in one
video. We're going to cover your social
media toolkit, how you can make shoots
and campaigns pop by your camera choice.
We're going to talk about cameras for
photo all the way from entry level and
fun to how to make your campaign smash.
I'm going to walk through a bunch of
what I own. Then we're going to talk
about cameras for video at all different
price points and levels that are built
for effective workflow. There'll be lots
of tips and tricks throughout this
video. Tons of stuff you may want to
come back to when you have some money to
spend. And I live in this world every
day. Argue about this, work with all
kinds of creatives. I had a lot of fun
deciding what to put in this. So I hope
you enjoy it as well. Let's lock in. So
first of all, why listen to me? So, I've
gone through every part of the gambit of
having to use equipment and creative
work. I started as a designer and
creative, had to take a lot of my own
shots. Then, when we had our own agency,
not only do we have a video team, I was
both leading the team, helping pick the
gear and the workflow and having to
shoot myself and deal with all the
process that went around that. And then
now I rarely shoot at all, but I keep a
full toolkit to help make the shoots I
am on as platinum as possible cuz often
I'm booking newer or younger
photographers, want to be able to hook
them up with great gear or I'm going on
a shoot we've already booked and I want
to have stuff in my bag of tricks to
make it pop. And overall with the
creative strategy work I do with brands
where we're helping them try to make the
best social media content possible. I
and all the people I worked with are
obsessed with workflow. How do you get
the most out of the least amount of
money? How do you shoot the most in
camera and not have to worry about post?
Now, how do you get the most bang for
your buck in terms of social media
performance? And I'm stoked to break

going to start with the funnest part.

any camera, any photographer. you're

the stuff that is actually in like true

looks that much better. And the answer

medium format. So, this right here is
the Pentax 645Z. It's one of my favorite
cameras I own. Is probably the cheapest
medium format camera you can get. Medium
format just has a different look than
any other camera on the market. It
immediately looks more editorial. It
looks more professional. You can tell
when you see people shooting with it.
It's why their stuff looks better. But
it requires a lot of knowledge. This is
a hard camera to use. It is heavy. It is
complicated. It is slow and it is old.
And if you don't really understand

645 manual Pentax lenses from back in

75mm f2.8 which is like $200. This is

when you are shooting with prime lenses
at a low f-stop. If you don't know what
that means, this is probably not the

Actually, you know what? I'll give you
like the quick crash course right now.
So, your aperture, how much light is let
into the camera, functions on a system
called f-stop, up to a number like 16 or
11, that's high, all the way down to a
low number. The lower it is, the more
light is being let into the camera. But
you don't care about any of that. All
you care about is what it looks like.
When you get to a low number, like this
is an f28, this is an f12. These really
low numbers mean that a very, very small
amount of your shot is in focus. It has
a very shallow depth of field. You also
get what's called bokeh, which is that
effect around whatever is in focus that
has these very like kind of interesting
textured effects to it. The lower that
you shoot, you shoot on that f12, etc.,
the less is in focus, that's how you get
that really cool effect you've seen in
so many photos you may not be able to
replicate. And you may not be shooting
fully all the way down, but say you're
shooting like an f2.8 or an f4. That's
how you get that really strong
separation between a subject and its
background. versus if you're shooting at
a higher f-stop, that's where everything
is going to look as if it's equally in

## 17. Building a brand aesthetic (real life collection design)

So, in this video, we're going to walk
through creating a brand aesthetic. And
I'm going to go entirely through a
capsule that I just creative directed.
We made some really nice items. Things
like this Togo leather duffel bag, these
wax cotton hoodies. We've gotten tons of
appreciation for on the short form
videos I've worn it in. Custom
envelopes, custom packaging, cashmere
hats, silk scarves, the whole gambit.
I'm going to walk through how something
all this comes together from mood board
to reality and how you have the
aesthetics that actually tie it into
something special. How you design
collections overall. We'll start with
the graphics and inspiration. We'll move
into the actual collection design and
the product design. I'll unbox the
entire collection so you can actually
see all the details, the things we put
together. And then we'll go into the
content and photos we made and some
strategies for when you're approaching
collections and putting these things
together yourself. So, Hyper, which is
my newsletter I run with another
creator. If you're not familiar with me
at all, I'm Orura Mates World. I'm a
designer, creative director, former kind
of marketing head who makes a lot of
content and does a lot of design in the
luxury products and fashion space. And
my partner inside of Hyper is Clayton
who runs which is like a men's fashion
newsletter. He previously worked at
Barney's and his has a very different
but equally as intense vision as I do
about what makes things interesting and
luxury and stand out in branding and
design. So we write our brand strategy
newsletter every week um on hyper and
talk about all kinds of things and then
we do projects like this where we offer
creative direction design etc for brands
that are trying to do interesting
things. So we got approached by Fermont
who are a landing page company and
basically funnels and similar things for
e-commerce. They do software where they
want to make a luxury merch collection
to be able to give to their best clients
to be able to offer for sale and be able
to showcase their technology which
allows people to create like custom
pages and full funnel flows for their
e-commerce brands. So from there it
starts with a mood board. So Clayton uh
put together this mood board of what
we're looking at for the collect gift

are going to be involved to give them a

this first image is kind of a perfect

align of like where we have this kind of
elevated men's wear feel but it still
has like some level of casual street
wear to it. Things that are more
subdued. You see the overall palette
elevation in kind of materials texture.
You'll see some of these recurring
themes we end up with the palette really
similar to the two items this guy is
wearing here. Some similarities in that.

think about the collection and what we
can do. So it starts there just to get

that's there which we did not want to

We'll go back and forth really think
about where can we apply things
correctly. So our next step here was
kind of collection design and structure
overall. So, how I typically look at
collection design is that you need your
standout item, your your viral items for
lack of a better term. What are the
things that are going to get people's
attention? That's category one. And for
us, it was this waxed hoodie where I
knew as soon as you put it on like
social media video or you see it in real
life, you go, "Wow, it's going to be
crazy." I knew I wanted to do it. I knew
the factory and the places that would
have the treatment. We'll unbox it a bit
later, but I was like, "This is going to
be our our standout." And so, how do we
want to apply iconography there? We did
an embroidery hit in the front,
embroidery hit in the side, and then we
decided to do a silicone tag on
everything. As far as the apparel items
to help like tie it together, you know,
in the collection, category one is those
standout items, the things that make
everyone want that they're going to be
that are going to sell out quick that
people are going to be asking about. How
do I get my hands on? The second one was
this leather bag, which we will unbox
here in a bit. I knew we wanted to do a
duffel. I've been working on some duffel
tech packs with the team. And so was
like, hey, let's do something that's
really high-end for that, too. Then you
have your core items when it comes down
to structure. So like a shirt is a core
item. For us, we also wanted to balance
affordability because some of this was
being given away and some was being
sold. You also got scarves which are a
lower price item. And then these Kashmir
hats, another one where like we felt the
shirt and the hats are the core items
and those should be kind of what's going
to be most consumed.
I think that breakdown is how most
people should look at collection
designs. So, if you're starting small,
for most people, I'd recommend starting
small and you have about five items. I'd
be really shooting for two to be like
extremely stand out to have two that are
more normal and easy to get and have one
that's like cheaper or an easy access
point where you can buy in or get into
the brand with an accessory or similar.
So you have a small collection kind of
to ratio like we put two standout items,
two normals items, one accessible.
That's a pretty decent way to start. But
as you get bigger, you expand the middle
and the bottom like for those
collections getting those real standout
items especially if they're more
expensive or you want people to be like
hunting after them. I will kind of keep
it in that ratio until you're looking at
like two to eight to two if you're
looking at like a 12piece collection
where you have like two standout to like
eight solid across a range to two more
accessible. Uh and then that's where you
can start being like all right maybe
let's add a third standout item. But
that's kind of the ratio I like to look
at is even when you're small you still
need those standouts and then as you
grow growing the middle of the things
actually people use and wear and then
keeping those like outsides a little bit
small and this is corporate merchandise
so a bit different than a full fashion
line and full fashion collection.
There's a whole bunch of thoughts into

for a while, people showing off their
their wealth by spilling out of their

that trend was then based on a like 90s

their influencers their customer people

120 days to do something like this
effectively. And some people will tell
you that that's really fast because you
could obviously be planning collections
like a year in advance. Uh then some
people will tell you that's slow because
if you did everything at the highest
price point possible and aired
everything, you could probably do the

running a little bit more risk. I think
120 days is the perfect amount of it. I
hope you enjoyed walking through this

the end result of what everything looks

## 20. Choose One of these Archetypes to Beat 99% of Brands on Social Media

In this video, we're going to talk about
a secret weapon for brand social media,
archetypes. Archetypes are about
deciding the role that a brand plays in
a consumer's world and how they come
across on social media. It becomes
basically a template for the kind of
content you create to get a strategy
that works. In a previous video, I
talked about how to start brand social
media from scratch if you have nothing.
But for this archetypes workshop, we're
going to ask the question about what do
you do if you've already started on
social, you have something going, but it
just doesn't do good enough. As
someone who has worked through this now
with hundreds and hundreds of brands in

do, I've built methods for this and I'm
going to teach them all here. We're
going to walk through how to do this
exercise. We're going to walk through
five archetypes that apply to the
majority of brands. Now, I have examples
in here for software, fashion,
interiors, resale, food and beverage,
and services. You have a wide swath. No
matter what kind of business you have,
there's no excuse not to tap in. So,
after we go through the exercise itself,
we're going to walk through each of
those archetypes, what brand they're
good for, the question to ask yourself,
the type of content they make, and
examples of brands that are doing the
strategy. No fluff, all tactics. And as

and I'm happy to workshop live in the
next community call we have, which I
will link down below for any projects

I love exercises. I love a series of

to use and have conversations about with
your team. And you can stop kind of
floundering around, stop trying to be
everything to everybody, and lock in on
one archetype. Hope you found this
helpful. As always, thank you so much
for watching.

---

## 22. Developing taste is easy, actually

There's an entire cultural conversation
now around the idea of having taste.
Tech firms are desperate to integrate
people into their creative departments
who are tasteful. A new generation is
exposed to more and more online and
learning how to decipher it. And
contrary to popular belief, developing
taste is easy. And in this video, we're
going to talk about how to find your
taste. Because while it is easy, it is
not innate. It is completely learned and
it is a lifelong journey, but one you
can really enjoy. All it takes to
improve, find it for yourself, is just
the start. In this video, we're going to
talk about the foundations of taste,
frameworks for finding your own,
learning how to critique and decide if
things are good and bad in the society
that's taught us not to. We're going to
talk about how your taste is reflected
in your decisions, how to really find
your taste in the real world, and the
progression of establishing taste over
time. I'm excited for it. Hope you all
enjoy the ride. [ __ ] it. But we're in
this time where tech in particular has
discovered taste and finally has some
value in it. Before it was almost a
counter signaler to have any care about
anything in the world except a
technological goal. But now in a world
that's more visual than ever, where
people have more standards than ever and
where content is educating them more
than ever, taste is ultra important. We
see everything from claw positioning its
entire brand in the AI space based on
taste doing their pop-ups to large
acclaim. Now, exposing a tech world to
marketing the rest of us have seen for
decades to financial brands like what
Quarter has done where they've applied
interesting visuals and the basics of
creative direction to create something
tasteful and unique that better reflects
their product and helps differentiate it
from the old guard. But how do you
actually learn it? I want to talk about
the foundation. There are two levels to
the foundation. One is the concepts of
human perception and then second is the
foundational knowledge of what's been
established through history. And these
both play a role with each other. But
we're going to break all the
fundamentals of taste into a couple core
categories. Visual, audio, and
experience in particular. And then in
categories like books, fashion, and
music. But it's important to talk about
the visual nature of things because this
is a framework from which taste starts.
So if you have ever been into
photography, you've heard of the rule of
thirds. I'm going to use this as an
example as there are a number of
basically immutable laws that are around
us that help determine if we like
something whether we know it or not. The
composition of a photo, the grid system
which controls typography and the
layouts of how anything visual we see,
whether we find it appealing or not,
whether it just feels wrong or feels
unprofessional or just resonates without
even knowing. And these have been

I want to try this like I recreated this
shot in a shoot recently or think about
doing a filter like this. I'm shooting
some vehicles in the future. Like, okay,
how can we put our own spin on making
like a compilation of something like
that? Like I have these these big boards
of things I want to try for shoots.
while at the same time when you're
working on a brand identity in another
project. I was saving a bunch of things
for that pitch just in this one simple
font board. But if you have those, it
gives you a framework to consistently be
adding to. If you are creative and
you're working creative, I would have a
couple different buckets there. So, I
would have a board for every brand and
every project that you're working on,
you can add stuff to. I have a couple
overall boards. Typography, no matter
what your life is going to interact with
type, I would be saving some typography
boards. Same thing with ads or
inspiration by industry if you work in
multiple industries. And then just a
personal board of just things you like.
Having some big setups. I have a few of
these. We can just save stuff that is
special to you for whatever reason so
you don't lose it in your head later on
is a great way to set up. And what I
usually end up doing is every Sunday
night, one of the things I used to do to
combat like Sunday anxiety is I would
organize all my bookmarks for the week.
So Sunday nights I would go through my
Twitter, Instagram, Tik Tok, whatever it
was, and I would put my bookmarks into
my boards or into action items in my
to-do list. It was a calming thing for
me that made me feel like I'm getting
ahead. got some value and also man I
didn't lose all those references you
just save in your bookmarks in
perpetuity. That's always an exercise I
do and if you are doing those things
where every week you're adding your
references somewhere and in free time
instead of scrolling you're like mood
boarding and adding stuff to these
important boards you'll find when you
show up into creative conversations or
go to do a project you have this massive
base that you've built that allows you
to really be able to crush things out
and even when you're building pitches
for clients I recommend think about the
same way. You don't want to be one big
mood board. Build a smaller board for
type doesn't take a ton of items to get
your point across. You can have five 10
things on a mood board. Like even three
to seven things that are perfectly
positioned is like super useful. The
clarity of thought by a mood board for
type, a mood board for color, a mood
board for packaging allows you to remove
that like indecipherable like oh I kind
of like this with a little bit of that
like feedback. It's like okay well let's
get feedback on the type like which of
these three images matter the most. Like
there's the ability to kind of get more
concrete. You'll see um you're working
with big web firms, you're working with
folks that are going to be dialing into
lots of smaller mood boards like inside
what they ask for feedback for. That's
one of the best tools you have is like
send somebody a mood board, allow them
to remove the items that they don't like
or get feedback on it and they can come
up with boards that allow you to
navigate through what people like and
don't and how you actually bring that
creative vision to life. So anyway, if
you want to explore more, this ties
really well into the art direction
videos I do. I've got one on road that I
think is underrated. Also got one on
Stuy that I imagine most of you all have
seen. You can dive in more into art
direction in those videos there. You
have any questions on this, you have any
other tools other people should know
they may want to see in the comments,
please drop below. And I appreciate
y'all so much. Uh, please drop a like,
subscribe, all those things. And thank
you so much for watching.

## 28. How Dupes are Exposing Luxury (LARP economy explained)

There's a trend going on on social
media, but it's spilling over into real
life, too. Larping, or live-action
role-playing. Not the medieval cosplay
or bardcore, but basically the act of
making fun of the influencer world while
also participating in it. The posts
about it are massively popular, 1.8
million likes. But it ties to a bigger
trend that we're seeing about all of the
consumption we have. The knockoff brands
becoming the most popular brands, the
duplicate replacing the original, the
advent of luxury with this new premium
boring that makes the world go round. In
this video, we're going to talk about
it. We're going to dive into why this
larping trend came up. We're going to
talk about why this actually happened in
luxury consumption over the last few
years and where it went wrong. The rise
of the dupe economy, how influence
around buying expensive things has
shifted. And then we're going to talk
about the brand recipe and brands that
are executing this well, or succeeding
inside this economy where we're more
price conscious and realize you can get
these aspirational items for a lower
cost. And I'm going to end with the
playbook if you are a marketer or
developing a brand and you're working in
this niche, how do you position to
actually succeed? So whether you're a
consumer or marketer, or just interested
in how these trends are shifting the
world around us, let's lock in. So
first, how do we get to this how to larp
like a pro series? How do we get to
everyone saying, "Oh, I'm going to buy
replica Lamborghini car keys online. I'm
going to visit a local car dealership
just to take pictures. Turo the
Maybach." The core seller larp, but now
brought as an activity to show up on
feeds. I do appreciate the empty
designer bags from Etsy. So it started
with everyone was marketed luxury,
especially in COVID and after. It was
the first time online there were lots of
people talking about these brands.
People were introduced to Rimowa
suitcases. Goyard was taking off in
popularity, a lot because of its dupes,
which we'll talk about later. People
really into lifestyle influencers. When
everyone disliked their life or was in
lockdown, they were looking to other
more interesting lives. Social media and
video, especially, exposing us to rich
people who'd never really been online
like that before. And it was different
than watching the Kardashians on TV
because it seemed like people just like
us. It seemed like they were just
spending more on those things. And this
led to a lot of consumption. Luxury
brands have raised their prices and
their sales have gone through the roof
because it became normalized to buy
these things that really only the 1%
should be purchasing. There is no world
in that if you are not in that bracket
of consumer, you should be buying a
suitcase that is over a thousand
dollars. But people do buy them. In my
friend group, we refer to it
affectionately as the Rimowa effect
because it then had this fallout. People
spent this money on these luxury items
and then they realized that they weren't
worth it. Rimowa's the perfect use case
cuz anyone who's actually used those
know they dent super easily. They suck.
Yes, they have lifetime repairs, but
you're using that lifetime repair. It
simply is not worth the bulk and the
quality to have for anyone that doesn't
have a huge amount of expendable income
where they don't even have to care. And
it's a reminder every time you use it
that you chose to waste that money on a
status symbol. Whether it's that or
another purchase, so many people had
that experience. When you go to actually
buy something you needed, you'd be like,
"Man, I really shouldn't have gotten
that $700, $900 pair of shoes, or
whatever it was." And while it shot up
luxury brand sales, so many new people
were participating in it, also raised
awareness that there really is no
difference in those goods. And now
LARPing has taken this full circle.
Because in the LARPing world, and this
happens in tons of influence online, the
goal was attention, right? The goal is
to get attention from whoever on social
media by using expensive things to do
it. One of the cheapest forms of
attention, but it works. But they don't
care by what means that they achieve it.
This is where we enter into the dupe
economy. So, I remember when I was in
China in Guangdong, going through all
the different cities, the quality of the
dupes was incredible. We would be in
factories that were quite literally as
you see it described online where
they're making shoes for clients I have
or brands like the things I operate on
one side of the warehouse, on the other
side of the warehouse they are churning
out Yeezys or whatever. And then there,
very easily you can get delivered to you
the highest quality of dupes. I remember
we would purchase some dupes of things
that we had to compare the quality cuz I
was going to do a video on it. And we
actually laughed because one of the
bags' quality for a YSL bag was higher
than the actual one. And you've seen the
same thing with lab diamonds, right?
Where the cost uh I was there in China
in the jewelry city in front of the lab
diamond machines. It was massive machine
about the size of like the height of
this room that just spits out a diamond.
And when you put that into perspective
of what that means for a cost, you begin
to look at everything significantly
differently, especially when they are as
good as anything {quote} {unquote} real.
And so it was different when it was a
cheap duplicate. When it's an equal
duplicate, you begin to realize you're
purely paying for the status. And when
you don't get the status because so many
other people have it because larping is
taking off. That leads to a very
positive consumer shift. Are you buying
for the experience or for the attention?
And if it's the attention, then larping
wins because it's more effective. You
can do more. The dollar goes longer. And
that attention is now worth very, very
little. And that is where we are at
today. We are at the precipice of this.
It doesn't matter if your Goyard is real
or not. There are so many fake ones.
That wallet is the most duplicated item
with the least amount of fighting by the
original brand to try to get it taken
down cuz they like the popularity. It
has no signal. And so now people know.
People don't look up to those luxury
influencers the same way. So one of the
big influencer shifts we've seen in just
the last year is that all of the like
perfect aesthetic life influencers, male
and female. You would see there's guys
who are really stylish doing get ready
with me in Dior or look at these new
expensive perfumes I bought. And their
entire feeds are really about
consumption of expensive things. And
they're showing that off in these
aesthetic ways. And they're really
offering you very little value beyond
that. They're not being like, "Let me
explain to you my thoughts on
Nietzsche." Or "Here's these really
valuable tips for your life or your
career." They are showcases of
consumption. There's a obviously whole
genres of female aesthetic influencers
who just showed off their kind of
beautiful perfect lives. And that
content has fallen really flat. And the
last content left is people like
I get sent this guy all the time. This
is a poor person's version of what a
rich person does every day. Yeah, I'm
going to purchase this new Rolex like
inside my G Wagon. Every single thing on
here is an expensive purchase. Let me
show you these Bottega shoes without any
semblance of why they're consuming, what
they're doing it for, any reasoning, any
backups of life or story, any any reason
to connect to the person beyond the
aesthetic of what they're presenting,
which is the most lowbrow form of
expensive consumption. All kudos, an
amazing creator making videos that get
consumed quite a lot, but an example of
the only things that are left in that.
And they are not selling to the market
that can afford it or would want it. But
this is the opportunity, and the
opportunity for the brand space is now
the consumer is aware that they are
being taken advantage of. They've made
those mistakes, and now they actually
want a good thing at a good price, which
is the right way to consume. And there
are a lot of brands, I'm about to walk
through a whole list of them, that
actually do this very well, and some of
them that do it a little more sketchily,
and we'll break down the difference. And
that's an opportunity, whether you're
consuming or whether you're building, to
be able to get outsized value. And so,
one story I will tell about this is I
was at a friend's house in Beverly
Hills, and they had this coffee table
that I have seen online on like First
Dibs or whatever. And it's really
expensive, and I was like, "Oh, like,
where did you get it? Original piece,
whatever." And they'd gotten it from The
Essential. And The Essential has a lot
of the pieces that it's hit expiry on
the design in the United States, so that
anyone can produce it, and they will
make a version of it at a more
affordable price. And we'll get to
brands like Interior Icons like too far
a little bit later. Such as like a good
in between. But it was funny cuz I had
not heard of it. I ended up actually
talking to the team from there
relatively shortly after. But it was
this idea that this is a pretty like
abstract item. This is a like what I
like to call this one in particular is
it was in a home surrounded by other
interesting and expensive things. And
they're like, "Yeah, I actually can't
get this other version I want, and I was
able to get it here." And you were able
to like achieve this like interesting
look. But that's the business, right?
The business of what are these amazing
artistic things, and if you have an
idea, bringing it to market in an
affordable way, because we now have that
ability. Or looking at what are all
these expensive things that are being
produced that don't have to be
expensive. We'll get to a playbook and
how you do that in a moment. But Keens
is the ultimate example of this, right?
A little hit or miss in terms of the
quality, but their entire brand exists
to say that things you used to have to
pay an awful lot of money for, you can
now get at these lower prices. And
especially for bedding, home goods,
travel, and now furniture, they have
options across everything. And they will
showcase the price you save versus
traditional retail, right? And they are
just taking advantage of direct to a
factory and they are marketing it that
way. What they've done is gone wide.
Once they're known for a few good
things, they've said, "Great, we have
this customer base that has in mind that
we offer them a great value where
they're not getting taken advantage of
and they still get good quality. And we
have factory relationships. It doesn't
matter what we sell. Our brand isn't
based around the fact we sell sheets or
homes. We can really sell anything. Our
goal now is to raise the LTV, lifetime

up and you're like, well, actually you

perception you have of maybe that older
Gen X woman wants to be that younger Gen
Z woman. You begin to start noticing
these shifts of, okay, we're marketing
to one person, but the customer that
attracts actually has a different
desire. Or our marketing isn't
connecting cuz we're actually marketing
to the wrong person. The person actually
ends up buying us isn't even discovering
it through our funnel. This is how you
can begin to have those conversations
that are important. So you start with
your persona knowledge. And then you
begin to think about signature designs.
What you'll notice about all the
identity marketing products that we
talked about, whether that's a No walla
water bottle, whether that's a Rep
T-shirt, whether it's the choice of your
company to be a Google versus a Teams
organization, all of these have visual
signatures. It could be colorways. It
could be form factors. When Dior had
cosmetics that went viral, it's cuz they
had basically like a little Dior
keychain attachment to it. It would
signal that you're a Dior person, give
you a luxury signaler from their their
logo on a low-cost purchase. It's an
identity play. Patterns, tags, like
subtle things like you see with
Margiela. I did this video with Grant
Owens, I got a lot of comment on my Bad
Son jacket. You can immediately tell
it's Bad Son because they have that
particular raised stitching pattern that
they do. These are all visual
signatures. They can apply to basically
anything. So people will associate
themselves with those brands. Color does
this as well. When I was in the toy
category, Nerf associated itself with
orange. Zuru was competing with them and
owned red. At Gel Blaster, hyper-focused
on green. You could see in big color
blocking on the shelf. You want people
to know, not get confused about which
you're buying. You want to be able to
go, "Oh, I'm going to get the green
one." These are little things that are
associations cuz they want to associate
themselves with whatever the marketing
was. I was recently talking to a luxury
grocer doing meal prep and they were
talking about packaging as a
differentiator. And everyone has the
same meal prep packaging and I was
telling them that they should go matte
green cuz you could still do that in the
same compostable style. Silk or do like
a emboss or deboss of their logo on it
and they would probably quadruple the
amount of people taking pictures of it
versus of the plain plastic top. It
would become an identity play to signal
that you got that meal kit because it
would look sick. And stick to that
demographic who's buying that kind of
luxury thing. And you could also do this
with iconography. Obviously, the logos
tell this story. And you'll see it white
fox, scuffers, all like the hip brands.
It's the easiest way to play it. Nude
project, iconography and logos tend to
work in some regards, especially with
younger demographics, but the more
subtle way is in kind of more different
differentiation when you're working on
color and pattern and things like that
end up being kind of usually bigger,
more sophisticated ways to do it over
time and in more categories. And then
I'm going to walk you through five
archetypes for lack of a better term.
Basically, five reasons that people will
associate themselves with identity for a
brand. So, first is belonging. I want to
fit in with people like me. This is
sneakers. Sneakers basically only exist
to find other people that will think
that your sneakers are cool. And by
doing that, you immediately have a
friend or have someone that will think
like have some respect for you and it's
completely about belonging. Every single
one of those purchases is around that.
And that's an excellent way to for a
very lonely demographics, right? And
sneakers appeal a lot to lonely people.
It becomes an obsession point. Fashion
in general is a large a larger extension
of that. To be able to find people that
have that same interest or that same
thing becomes a very easy signal. And
then there's a second out there. It's
hey, I'm better than the others. That's
another thing. There's a lot of people
that want to prove they're better than
other people even subtly. You have
brands like The Row that position
themselves entirely. I'm more tasteful
than everyone else in my friend group. I
have The Row playlist. I have a bag that
not everyone would know. I have this
subtle look. It's the it thing. You are
proving something to others and making
people aspire to what you have, but
subtly. Then you have the rebel. I don't
play by your rules. If you're marketing
to Gen X, this is like your go-to. I'm
going to choose not just the more
extreme angle, but the more outsider
angle than anything else. Which is
Liquid Death to some regard. There's
room inside kind of like a
counterculture rebel take in every
product category. Then you have
standout. You have flare. The I'm
special call. Look, that bad sun jacket
is flare. I own that jacket cuz I know I
can go on a podcast and it'll stand out
on a feed. is a flare piece. You only
have things like that if you are trying
to prove a point about standing out from
something. There are plenty of brands
that say something about you in a loud
way. And then there's the idea of pride
in a subculture. And if you know, you
know. And you can base that around
geography, around race, around hobbies,
around gender, but saying that you're
proud of your background. That yes, of
course I'm going to have this
Chinese-founded brand or whatever it is
that tells that story to a subculture.
Those are just five that you can work
with. Fitting in, being better than
somebody, not playing by anybody's
rules, standing out, or having pride in
a subculture. It's an amazing way to
just begin thinking about how an
identity marketing playbook could work
for any product. I want to end with
identity beyond affiliation. Cuz
identity can also be deeply personal. It
doesn't have to do anything with others.
Almost all of those have to do with a
signal you're sending to others. But one
of the core points to think about in
marketing is if people have a weak point
in their internal identity, how can you
help resolve it? And a great example of
this was uh I worked for years with Lift
Foils on their marketing. They're a surf
foil company, electric hydrofoils, etc.
And we had a big advocate, a customer
advocate. Drew Brees. He's a famous
quarterback. He bought this product.
While I was running marketing there, we
had a massive gift given to us that he
went on Ellen after he stopped being a
quarterback. He talked about one of his
favorite things to do was ride on his
efoil. And he actually did like a
training with Ellen. And it was a huge
sales process for us, but not just
because a celebrity was showing it off.
Because of what it solved. Not to get
too deep into hydrofoils, but basically
you have classic hydrofoils that have
basically a mast and you ride waves and
there's no motors or anything on there.
And it's a work of modern physical
mastery to be able to pump and foil. I
have 14 stitches in my forehead from a
foil, so my my days of any of these are
gone. But then the electric hydrofoils,
you're basically flying on water, and
you have a motor and a remote control,
and it's an incredible feeling and
experience. But for either of those
product, but in particular the motorized
one, there's a lot of guys who get to a
certain age, and they have the same
experience as me. I got 14 stitches in
my head from a hydrofoil. It was proof
to me that yeah, I'm not young anymore.
I can't do extreme sports anymore. It
was like my third injury in a row. I
fell off an ATV. I had bruised a rib
snowmobiling. I was just I was like,
"Okay, I still think I'm 22, and I'm
not." Happens to a lot of guys. But you
see a product and you're like, "Hey, I
can still have that experience. I can
still have that feeling of flight, of
exhilaration, but I can do it in this
really modern way, and that's a problem
I can solve with money that's really
cool, that someone I respect, a pro
athlete, is doing." That solves a weak
point in so many guys' identities who
had made that water sports a core part
of what they do, and who could continue
doing that in a way that was cool and
fun. And uh and this ties into kind of a
last strategy I'll outline here about
about testing that. Because what was
interesting there is we tested all kinds
of ads, right? I was in the same
framework I talked about how when I was
talking about HubSpot earlier, what I
did I ran all the meta with an agency
inside there. We worked on ad creative
and collateral, etc. I worked with
Vervont on that. Shout out Vervont. But
we were testing traditional, like, "Hey,
let's get our our team on there. Let's
get We have We have pro surfers. We have
We have Laird Hamilton. We have Brian
Grubb. We have a lot of pros we would
have in the ad account." And let's test
people who look like our customer
avatar. Let's test older surfers. You
would get this wide swath of different
consumer types like inside these ads
like to see what worked or not. And we
found that it's not the person that our
consumer wanted to see themselves in.
They didn't necessarily see themselves
on the board. Some people did, but a lot
of them wanted to see the younger
avatar. They wanted to feel like they
were the younger person. It was like a
50/50 split. And those would go to
different landing pages, different
experiences, people that bought for
different reasons. And that's what I
would encourage you to test when you are
inside building ads, building organic
concepts. Think about who is your actual
demo, and who does that demo think they
are, and then who do they want to be,
and then who do they want to hear from?
A friend of mine has a a brand that
sells to elder women. He actually found
that young UGC from like younger
good-looking guys worked a lot better
for him than that demographic being
shown to themselves. You might find a
mix of that. But that combination of
what those archetypes look like, why
people buy, and who they want to buy it
from adds up to sophisticated marketing.
And if you're in one of these funnels
and you're being advertised, do you
start thinking about what does it say?
If it's actually solving an internal
problem that you have that is a weak
point for you, that's a good thing.
You're being shown a mirror of something
you may want that may actually solve
something for you. Or if you're being
shown something that's selling you a
version that feels kind of dirty, that
you don't quite want or feels off,
that's a bad sign for a brand. And the
more conscious we are on how we're being
sold and why and why we buy, the better.
So anyway, I'm excited to be cutting
some videos from the French Riviera here
next week with a bunch of the people
that I'm inside our Cannes house with.
In the house we have Dara
Denney's there, JT Barnett's there,
Sammy Cohen's there, Tayden Brandt, a
bunch of creators you may or may not
have heard of. We're making all kinds of
interesting content we have scheduled
depending on timing, and so I'm excited
to drop some of that on this channel
too, so you can see some more
conversations over the coming weeks. I'm
working on a video about actual French
luxury as well. So for those of you
waiting for more luxury content, that is
coming soon. And as always, thank y'all
so much for watching.

Really, I feel like the best way to

brand-focused for the last two eras.
We're going to go through terminology.
I'm going to discuss the difference
between the strategy, content
operations, and creative directions. The
terms get used interchangeably
quite a bit. We're going to zone in on
the personality era, how brands use
personalities and why they need to be
creative directed by the brand.
World matters more than the account now.
Creating a content flywheel and
determining how to actually put content
out around personal brands from a
creative director perspective.
Distribution strategy and how to apply
creative direction principles to
what personal brands do. And then get
into the actual sauce of how you apply
creative direction techniques
around visuals, design, styling, and
world-building to personal
brands. This is a topic that is near and
dear to my heart as someone who has
built my own personal brand but hundreds
of success stories come out of Cut 30.
And in particular, this last week I was
at Cannes Lions here in the South of
France. I'm still in Nice right
now where creators and personal brands

largest advertising and
creativity festival. The entire industry
was gathered here and the number one

personality era we're in. I'm
inside this every day as someone who's
worked on this formally inside a brand.
I'm excited to share as much sauce as I
possibly can with y'all inside
this video. Let's lock in. But first,
let's talk about the creative direction
eras. The era number one was the
original fashion and agency era. When
you thought of a creative director, you
would think of someone who worked inside
of a fashion house or someone who worked
at an agency. This was my first gig
inside this world. I was a graphic
designer who became a senior graphic
designer. I was at a smaller agency that
needed that differential. They were
trying to work with interesting clients.
At the time, we were with Red Bull, with

the term began to explode in what we

2.0, where all of a sudden Hypebeast,
Highsnobiety, all of these cultural

streetwear revolution and the

and mainstream, and gave this huge

these creative directors, at these
brands that made thousands and tens of
thousands of kids around the world
aspire to have those kind of positions.
Prior to that, when I was growing up,
people wanted to be athletes, they
wanted to be musicians. In this era,

I become a designer? Can I become a

things like Travis Scott with

it opened up the idea of the field to be
something much more. Why wouldn't
McDonald's want to activate with a

to tap into streetwear. And in this era,
I'd recently become a VP of marketing. I
was working at a company called 3DR at

was managing a super talented creative

the singular person who was giving
incredible ideation that separated our

## 35. How to Make a Marketing Plan (2026 Playbook)

In this video, we are going to talk
about how to make a marketing plan, a
2026 guide. As many of you know, I was
on tour earlier this year. We did two
stops in New York, Miami, Toronto, and
in each place, we had 40 brands come in
to do live workshopping around their
marketing plans for the year. I ran
through a series of five exercises, and
we had brands from small independents
all the way up to major airlines and
activewear companies, software,
workshopping how they can actually make
a difference in this really complicated
marketing climate. I'm going to walk
through all of those same exact
exercises here in this video. There is
downloads to accompany this with all the
worksheets we used, so even if you
couldn't make it in person, you can
still sit down and actually go through
it. I highly recommend doing this with
your notebooks out, actually walking
through it for your brand or one of your
clients, or just theoretically, and
really going through the exercise to
think about what you can do to make a
difference in marketing this year. And
then you can save this for any future
time you're doing year or quarterly
planning. They're a great set of
exercises to do. I'm super excited
because when I was on tour, I got to see
the actual differences and
problems that businesses had, what their
problems are really dealing with versus
just the things we see on the internet.
It's a lot different in real life. And a
big part of why I wanted to go was to be
able to learn what challenges people
actually have, so that I have things to
make content about that are really
relevant to you. I feel like we've
really nailed that here. Let's get into
it. Lock in. So first off, if you missed
last week's community call, you can see
the recording in the downloads below as
well. We will have another one coming up
in March, so please get on the list for
that. I love workshopping with you in
person and online. It's one of my big
goals for this year. Now alternatively,
if you want to see the new tour dates,
and you want to be a part of that tour
coming up, Air, who put together this
last one, now we're talking about what
the next dates are. Let us know the
markets you're in as we are trying to
plan that actively. But let's start off
by walking through the exercises that
we're going to go through today. The
first is we're going to walk through the
brand's marketing funnel. Look at what
are we good at today, what are we good
at tomorrow, and explain the funnel in
general. Then we're going to look at
this chart of all the different
marketing initiatives that are going on
in the world and why you would consider
doing them in the world where there's so

the content funnel and why content
marketing are so aligned now. We're
going to talk briefly about asset

has the ability to do more each year.
Sometimes you have to do more with what
you already have. Then we're going to go
through your year planning calendar. So
let's start with the marketing funnel
exercise. And this is something I think
any company, even individual, if you
market yourself or your service or your
offer or your creator should do. If you
aren't familiar with the marketing
funnel, this is how people go from
attention at the top. What does your
brand do to generate awareness? How do
you get more and more people aware of
what you do? And then how do you
graduate them down through the funnel,
get them educated on your brand and
considering purchasing what you do,
following you, etc. All the way to the
bottom where they actually make the
sale. And so the point of this exercise
and how it works is that every brand
should look at, what are we doing right
now at each of these three stages of the
funnel? At the top, what do we do right
now for awareness? How do we bring in
attention? And then the key question is,
are we actually good at it? Does this
work for us? Same thing in the middle.
If you're getting good attention, you
bring people in with viral videos or
ads, etc. Are you good at converting the
attention you already get into people
that are interested to buy? How do you
educate? How do you nurture? How do they
build affinity with your brand or your
product, whether it's software or
cosmetics? And then to the bottom, how
are we getting the sale? actually
pushing people through the funnel down
to a sale? And this is where I want to
stop for a second and outline that
funnels are very different for different
businesses and for different creators,
anything you do. So for instance, if you
sell a very mainstream product, you sell
Toyotas, you sell lipstick that every
woman could use, you sell guys t-shirts,
your top of funnel has a lot of
potential to get to your bottom of
funnel. You have a wide funnel. So a lot
of people come in at the top, a lot A
lot can exit at the bottom if you're
good because you have a big market for
your products. So, your job is to try
and get a lot of people really educated
and buying at the bottom, and you don't
want to have a ton of drop-off from the
top of your funnel to the bottom. A lot
of businesses are in a very different
position, where they have very specific
people they want to buy. One of the
people at our workshop was a software
firm that sells only to like ultra
wealthy businesses with super high
needs. Their contracts are seven
figures, and they're like, "How do we
play in this marketing funnel? We have a
such a small other end." And the
difference is, it's not actually that
different. It's just that way less
people come out the bottom. You need to
figure out a way to reach your target
demo, and maybe you can get ultra
targeted with that, but for a lot of
people it's going to be somewhat wider.
And your only care, whether you reach a
million people at the top or a hundred
thousand at the top, is how many people
come out the bottom. Are you getting to
the sale you want to get to? And it's
important concept to wrap your head
around, because some people may want to
go wide to get to small. But in this
exercise, we map out, "What do we do

to actually get people educated and
build affinity with our brand?" And so,

to the logo repetition in ads they're

## 39. How to build a luxury brand, pt 1

online culture in general has become
obsessed with luxury consumers are
interested in it Brands want to know how
to sell at luxury and premium price
points and it's something that I've
worked in almost my whole life but to
command those price points to be able to
call your product a luxury product to be
able to say I'm going to be able to
charge a significant worth you need to
have a certain set of principles that
your brand is going to be able to work
with and as a consumer you need to be
able to see these things and respect
what it is know whether or not it's
worth your dollar I'm going to Breeze
through a bunch of the brands who are
doing premium and luxury online Pres
business mostly on websites we'll test a
little bit on social throughout this
video and there's going to be a whole
series of different ways the brands come
across as luxury designer and premium
online one of the first Brands we going
to talk about is ESOP not just their
gorgeous website they have here but they
actually have an amazing design resource
called the taxonomy of design so this
website that you see here I'm scrolling
through is a catalog of every retail
store that they have and all the
materials and individual items used in
those stores this has come up pretty
frequently inside of projects that I've
worked on for interiors when we're
actually working on on the inside of
storefronts when we were working on
redoing our house because it highlights
all these individual features like these
really cool individual things that
people put together you can use for

## 41. How to build a marketing team in 2026 (content operations playbook)

In this video, we are going to cover a
full guide how to build a content-first
marketing team in 2026, no
matter what the size of your org is. So,
we're going to cover this from the
smallest to the largest of companies.
I'm going to go through examples, small
and large, to kick this off, all kinds
of brands that are going media company
first. We've all heard this trend, you
love to talk about on LinkedIn, every
marketing guru that brands are media
companies now, but no one talks about
how you actually execute it tactically.
After those examples, I'm going to talk
about how to build a marketing plan in
the content world. What does your team
look like? What are the goals to give
that team? What are anchors? What are
goals? Do you actually have the ability
to navigate if you're pivoting to this
new world, starting from scratch, or
just want to understand your place in
the world as a creative and a marketer
in this brand as media company era? And
I've been involved in this on every
level. I started as a content creator
when I was an SVP of marketing, and I
was pivoting my more traditional
marketing org to have to think like
this, and it was hard. Recently, I
advised on the build-out of an entire
studio system for an existing brand that
wanted to bring its expensive six-figure
productions in-house, working through
everything from their gear to the
facilities to what staff would look
like. And I saw that every single time
they brought in a traditional media
professional, the costs were going
radically out of control. It's a big
part of why I decided to make this
video. It's going to be super thorough.
I'm super excited for it. A lot of prep
went into this. Let's lock in. A quick
heads-up on the 24th, my next community
call, we're going to go through Q&A and
training just on this. If you want to go

right now most e-commerce stores are a

store. This near future of e-commerce is
beginning to happen right now. What's
called agentic shopping is where people
are going to interact with the site that
knows their budget, knows their sizing,
what they've navigated on before. It's
able to change in real time as they
move, answer their questions, and
present options. Give real advice on

hunt this stuff down, but they will sign
up for a newsletter to get sent directly
to them. Think in terms of lead magnets.
The idea of you give someone an email,
they get something from you in return.
Can you give them a one-page playbook or
a template or your compilation? Like I
have a lead magnet that's a packaging
directory. You may have seen this. It's
all my favorite packaging in like a
little notion database with links and a

because whenever I make a packaging

use it. So it took one time a lot of
time and now I'm going to use it for
literally years to get email
subscribers. But if you are posting
content on these other networks, you
need to ask for it. You need to have it
in your bio and kind of tie those dots
together. But that is the number one
growth, right? That drove probably
25,000 of the subs that we have
currently are like literally just from
that. And that is for anyone who's
creating that video content that is
worth doing. And then we have just
Substack in general. If you are on
Substack and you are getting recommended
by other people or you are recommending
or you are posting on there, there's a
lot of organic traffic that comes from
it. Clayton, my partner in hyper has a
men'sware newsletter called Spreza. He
has recommended by a lot of other
Substacks in that same niche. He
recommends other people. He grows
considerably every day. he wakes up and
has new subscribers from the fact that
he's just on Substack and in that
ecosystem. And this ties into this idea
that social media is social and email is
the same way. Work with other email
creators, cross collaborate, push each
other's newsletter. Like that is a thing
that works inside this industry. And the
last piece of this we'll talk about is

built-in thing for this where they'll
actually promote your newsletter to
other newsletters and you pay per
subscriber which a lot of people use.
And then people run Facebook and meta
ads, that same lead magnet we just
talked about where, hey, if I could run
a packaging directory ad that says, hey,
join my email list to get access to this
directory, I can probably get emails at
like less than a few dollars each. And
you begin to look at the economics of
this, getting the money next, and say,
hey, it's actually worth it for me to do
that because I make X amount over time,
you can begin to grow your list
monetarily. And there is so many of
these big newsletters you see, they've
grown this way, and it's somewhat

is this an organic? They really like me,
they really not. That's something you
have to test your way into finding out.

as fast as I want. Then the two things
you can do is say hey make the best
content ever that is going to go
basically sharable viral which is option

haven't done any paid in the newsletter
we have existing but if I was ever like
hey I I need to get this to 100k that's
the first lever I'd pull. So lastly
let's talk about making money on your
newsletter. So the first option is just
paid subscribers. So Clayton who runs
Presza he has paid subscribers hundreds

there's like a free newsletter every

and kind of filter that in." And you can
do very well on it. So when I was in New

has a a newsletter called the Sociology
of Business. She has a $50 a month
Substack, which I would not be surprised
if it has like thousands and thousands
of subscribers. And that is a good

she's providing genuine real value as
someone like noted with a super
impactful like deep dive article. So you
can work your way up from a couple
dollars to you know someone like feed me
who has way more subscribers but is
doing like you four or five dollars a
month to a more niche but doing like 50.
You have this variance in between. So
paid subs is number one. Again once you
have a newsletter if you're driving that
amount of value people like it that's an
easy way to turn on and and get a
baseline there. The second thing is your
actual offers. So, my friend James, who

talks about buying and selling
businesses. That's his entire thing,

that is promoted in the newsletter,
which probably would make way more money

He also has the same thing where if he

his offers through his newsletter, which
is going to be way better money than
paid subs, but you need to actually have
things that are worth selling and using
for this, right? This is an example like
on our hyper newsletter. We don't like
push a bunch of things, but I do have we
put out reports every so often. Like we

$200. You know, we don't push it really

Hyper runs a lot of ads. We have ads in

per. If you're in your niche, you may be

after that I was like Hey I want to get

## 49. How to make more money as a creative (offers playbook)

Today, we're going to talk about why
creatives are so afraid of money and
what you can do about it in your own
creative career. We are at a very unique
time in history. If you do any type of
creative work from design to videography
to copywriting, if you're in branding,
if you've ever thought of yourself as an
artist or artistic related, there is
more demand for that work than literally
ever before. But at the same time,
something I notice constantly with all
the creatives in my life is they feel
bad about financial success and they
feel doing the things, the actions that
turn their creative work into a
business, they feel almost guilty about
it. In this video, we're going to talk a
little bit about how you've been forced
into that by the functions of your
bosses and the psychology of capitalism
over time. But also, I'm going to give
you a direct blueprint. You're going to
learn about offers, you're going to
learn about funnels, we're going to talk
about CRM, and how to structure those to
make the most out of your creative work
in this unprecedented era. This video is
in partnership with Framer, so I'm
actually going to walk through how to
build websites around these as well. So,
you will have both theoretical and
tactical application of stuff that will
help you as a creative be able to make
more money and also not feel bad about
it. My goal for this video is to be the
most tactical video on how to structure
your creative business and get your
creative work that you've ever watched.
If you have questions about any of this,
please let me know cuz I want to do some
follow-up specifically about creative
money and creative work. I can dive into
it there. I'm excited for you to take
advantage of it in this unprecedented
time. Let's lock in. I don't usually
start my videos with other people's
videos, nor do I usually Mr. Wonderful,
but Kevin O'Leary had this quote that
was going viral that's really important
to what we're going to talk about here.
In this video, he talks about the
transition from the social media as an
intern job or one person to a companies
have increasingly realized that people
that understand the internet and can
help acquire customers, whether for
products, agencies, services, or
personalities, and the teams that do it
have become some of the most valuable
people inside of a company command far
more salary than ever. But more than
ever, people don't know what to charge
for that. They don't know how to ask for
that much money. They're caught inside
that barrier of what was my last salary.
And this is a function, in a large part,
due to the big agency multiplex. Small
agencies, you get less than 50 people,
they're just trying to get by. Big
agencies are structured like they are
for a reason. So you will see the
Omnicoms, the Saatchis, the Havases, the
large agencies of the world employ all
these different creatives and they put
them into silos. You're an art director,
but you don't really get to direct
campaigns. You're a copywriter, you're a
planner, you're a strategist. You
operate inside these little bubbles and
those bubbles are worth really specific
things by the number of accounts with
specific multiples for billable hours.
They're designed for the infrastructure
around you and the other jobs and the
profits at the top to take that money
and not the creative and they've held
people down for a long time. Now
historically, we want to date this back
to the origin of arts and commerce. If
you've ever looked at the book Caps
Lock, it details a lot about how graphic
design as a function and a lot of the
other artistic functions really came
apart as supporting the financial
infrastructure. Designs became necessary
for currency, for banks to help
establish credibility inside monetary
systems. Same thing with writing,
holding records for the exchange of
commerce. And even when artists became
great, it was patronage from the rich
that would allow people, like famous
artists of the past, even be able to
operate or scientists. The creative
relationship with money is a complicated
one for generations. And then we get to
a societal shift. In the 70s, in the
hippie era, all the way through the 90s
and into Gen X and grunge, art was
looked at as something that was
anti-selling out. If you participated in
this, you didn't need the man's money,
you didn't want their support, you
wouldn't take that McDonald's deal.
Anyone of my generation remembers Reel
Big Fish's song Sellout. It was a bit of
a rallying cry about the willingness or
lack of willingness to buy into the
commercial infrastructure of music. But
that changed and it changed because at
the end of the day, a lot of artists
want the ability to make a living off
their art. And there's been more demand
for it than ever because what sells
commerce in our world now, that is a
world of attention of undifferentiated
goods, is increasingly art. Whether it's
the cover art of an album, the music
associated with the lifestyle, the
design of a website, an ad that got
created by a creator, all these are
extensions of the creative field, and
they're all involved in the business of
making money. Meta ads allowed us to
quantify this. Tracking in UTM systems
to websites from social media content
allows us to quantify this. The best
thing that's ever happened for creatives
in terms of their fiscal goals in
alignment was TikTok Shop being able to
directly measure if your video sold
units of a product and assign that a
level of value. And Meta ads, where you
could say, "I made this ad or I created
this thing, and I can track how many
dollars that came back directly from
it." So, creatives who wanted to go
within that mad that mad space
infrastructure, all of a sudden realized
what they're worth, and it was way more
than the agency infrastructure was
paying. But all that is just background.
What most people have never heard, what
most creatives have never heard, that
I'm going to tell you right now before
we get into the actual tactical part of
the video, is a message I want you to
remember. Your skills are in the highest
of demand. In the world that we operate
in now, as long as you are willing to
apply yourself, quantify the work, and
actually lock in and don't be lazy, your
creative abilities can give you fiscal
and life success beyond what you can
dream of. Act on it. Never feel bad
asking for what you're worth. So, with
that, there is a gap. There's a gap in
learning for creative people to apply
this to business. Then there's a gap of
actually doing that application and
getting it done. And last, there's the
willingness to sell. I'm going to tackle
that last one first, and then we're
going to get tactical. So, first is the
willingness to sell, willingness to
charge a lot of money. So, first I will
say, if you knew what these corporations
have made off of the backs of creative
work in generations, you would be sick.
If you knew what other people made in
these companies versus what their work
is worth, you would be upset. Hold on to
that feeling. But second, creatives that
charge a lot for their work enable other
creatives to do it and fund other
creatives. Let me give you an example.
I've done a lot of shoots in my life. If
you advocate for a budget for a shoot,
the difference between me getting
150,000 and 250,000 to put together a
commercial shoot isn't money that goes
in my pocket. It's my ability to hire
more creatives to make a better product.
And you will see actual creative
operators, the majority of them look at
it like that. They know what they're
going to make roughly either way. Maybe
they'll make a little more, but they get
to employ more people to make better
art. If you are the kind of person that
helps command that that breaks out of
the mold, that's able to negotiate in
those factors, that brings that in, that
brings up the creative ecosystem around
you, every creative who's been around
for a while knows a few of those people,
and those people are great. They get you
the work when you need it. They help
you, they facilitate things. Do
everything you can when you do get that
power to be one of those people. But to
get tactical, first you need what's
known as an offer. And I want you to
forget about If you've read any info
product books, or if you look down on
the Hermozis or the Brunson's of the
world, I am not going full This is how
you sell guru. I'm breaking down the
basics of how every business works, and
you're going to find it valuable. So the
idea of an offer is what do you sell? So
most people will say, "I'm a designer,
I'm a copywriter, I'm a video editor,
I'm a marketer, I'm a brand strategist,
or a creative director. I can do all
kinds of things, right?" That's how you
approach your look on life. But if you
are trying to get clientele, you need to
be able to fill in this blank. I do X
for Y at Z. I make landing pages for
e-commerce businesses for $1,500 a
landing page. I do brand identities for
tech companies for $5,000 in 2 weeks.
That is an offer. It is a singular thing
at a set price for a niche. That is how
you translate from I'm a designer, I do
all sorts of stuff to I can fit a
consumer need. Cuz let me play out a
scenario for you of how this flow works.
You post on social media, or you get a
referral, or someone's looking at your
work, and they need that service.
They're looking for a specific thing, a
brand identity, something for their
brand. They go to your website, they see
a bunch of work, but they don't see any
pricing. So I don't know if you're
expensive or if you're cheap. And they
don't really and you'll see a list of
services but you don't really know what
it looks like or what it does. Your job
as a service provider is to communicate
that to them. And to make repeatable
things that you know and what to expect.
This doesn't limit you from doing all
kinds of other stuff once you're in
conversation but it makes it easy for
someone to buy. Makes it easier for
someone to go, I need a logo. It's going
to take me 2 weeks. It's going to cost
me X and I don't have to get in any
awkward scenario or try to book a call
to find out. Eliminate that distraction.
Productize just one core thing you do.
And again, you can still offer a whole
world around it but the magic of the
offer is you get to see if people care

customer. So you look at all the Framer
sites that I showed, you'll notice
there's lots of call to actions to book
a call or get a download or sign up for
the waitlist as getting people into a

And so in your VSL, you go through in a

and how things sell on the internet. And
then I'll introduce you to a concept
called offer stacking. So some of you
have heard, you've probably heard me

things from him. But then it's turned

I'm a videographer. Hey, I can run meta.
Hey, I understand VSLs and scripting and

Now I sell an offer where the sum of
that offer allows you to get paid more
than its individual parts." That's a
great way to think about how you build

this goes for the higher end as well.
Because if you're already a brand

## 50. How to make viral content for ANY business (full playbook)

This is how to make killer social
content for boring businesses. Time and
time again, whether it's people that are
in Cut 30, brands I talk to online, or
people that are starting a personal
brand, they're always saying, "Oh, well,
I'm an accountant. I'm a dentist. I have
a massage business. We make candles. How
could we possibly make interesting
social content?" And this is absolutely
the wrong mentality because there are
dozens of ways with which you can make
amazing, captivating content about the
most boring things. and we're going to
break down those strategies inside of
this video. I'm going to talk about how
to use exciting scenarios, how to put
strong takes out there that establish
personality, how to incorporate viral
vehicles, taking an education ccentric
approach, really leaning into lore and
depth inside of a niche, and using
things like formats and speed to stand
out. It'll be a fun video no matter what
your brand is or what you do. If you're
interested in making awesome social
content, there will be a lot to learn
throughout. Let's lock in. So, first I'm
going to start off by just rattling
through a number of ideas. I know can
work for businesses that want to make
content that stand out in the internet.
And the first is what I call a format
shakeup. You don't have to reinvent the
wheel when you want to make something
successful. So you could take the hot
ones format. Everyone knows it. You are
eating spicy chicken wings while
answering questions and you could do
that legal edition. Hot law came off the
top of my head. Sometimes sometimes you
win, but literally with legal questions.
Talk to people about why they shouldn't
have a beware of dog sign while engaging
in a experience similar to Hot Ones. But
there are hundreds of format shakeups
you can do that inherently are going to
make even something that might not be
the most interesting thing more
captivating to watch. The second tool I
think is really interesting is speed.
We've all seen normal real estate tour.
If you did the same thing in 10 seconds,
a speedrun of showing an apartment,
you're playing to the algorithm
strengths because it likes faster
content. But there's always the element
of can you do something people
understand faster than they're used to
it and make it an event that you are
doing it faster. That's why you'll see
things like 30 second makeup, one minute
recipes. Time is a vehicle. Speaking of
vehicles, I want to talk about the idea
of viral vehicles. And I don't mean
cars. I mean objects and things that you
can buy or build into your business that
will help guarantee you virality. For
instance, if you are a toy store and you
had a hydraulic press, all of a sudden
you were one of the more interesting toy
stores on the internet. Those videos do
super well. Naturally, people are used
to what they see and you're just
applying it to a different thing. And
that investment is likely worth it. If
you were to say, "Hey, we're going to
spend thousands of dollars on getting
not the hugest hydraulic press, but
something that works for our videos or
whatever that viral vehicle is." And
you'll see people who rent really nice

with a vintage car or whatever it is,
those are all expenditures that help

that for your toy store or whatever
brand it was, the result in terms of the
views you're going to get on social

hacks you can look at and say, "Hey,
maybe I don't want to invest tons of
money every month in complex shoots, but
I want to invest in that one thing that
I know every time is going to help hit
and go viral." And one more quick
technique before I get into the middle
of this video and the big bulk of like
the longer tactics is the this is
amazing approach or using your skills
for good. But how can you do something
with that skill set that people go,
"This is amazing." When they look at the
content, for instance, if you were a

interesting copywriting agency. All of a
sudden, you're showing your skills in a

viral to others. And it's interesting, I

what's the angle. I know not everyone

and we will see what ideas that we can

That's why Dropbox has been such a core

you can put your expertise in context of

The power of his content is that he is

Everyone wants to be everyone's friend.
People don't want to be too negative or

in context of recent events. Very
similar to the Gwagon attention

because you it shows your expertise of

mastering? Again, it doesn't have to be
