that like, "I'm mastering this thing.
I'm doing one a month, whatever." is a
huge benefit to basically keeping you
going and to begin to see the rewards.
And where people get this wrong is they
think, "I'm going to start doing this,
and I'm going to get a bunch of money
from it after 90 days." No. Put in the
year. When the year comes, you can start
to think about where the money comes
from. But you've now set your
foundation. If it happens before then,
awesome, but don't even worry about it
until you've hit that year mark. So,
you've gotten 365 days of work on it.
That is the mastery year. And now
zooming over to the money year. So, like
I mentioned, I had this year, adopted
some of these strategies when my son was
first born. Our money was disappearing,
and started with us breaking down why.
This is where I would start with you
trying to understand where money's at in
your life. There's a couple key buckets
you're going to get into. First is your
consumption of the casual things.
Clothing, stuff for home goods, et
cetera. We all spend a lot more money on
that because we're marketed to
constantly in everything we do. There's
Klarna and Affirm, and it's a non-stop
flow of influence, but none of it is
stuff we need. We have to actually
mentally wrap your head around that to
do something about it. Then we have
subscriptions. What software are you
using? You'll find people that are like,
"Oh, I have Netflix and Hulu and Disney
and that. Oh, they're only $20, $40,
$10." But it adds up. And you don't need
everything. What if you just had one for
3 months, then switch to the next one?
Going out and vice, drinking and related
is a huge one. But we need to tally up
where those expenses come from that are
in your life. Cuz this is about making
the most of the money you have, not
trying to make more. Cuz a lot of
people, the answer to their issues is in
this. Cuz I get a lot of questions, "How
can I do campaigns like this with my
brand? You need to be making way more
money." Or, "Hey, I don't have the money
to to start a brand or become this or
become that." It's mostly lying in side
what you already consume. Now, it's not
for everybody, but if you are in that
like 70K plus bucket, a lot of it comes
down to decisions. And you can begin to
change that by understanding it. So, the
exercise that I would lean into for one
full year is in your consumables, your
clothing, your home goods. Can you buy
nothing new? Can you survive just off
what you have? Maybe your essentials,
your socks, whatever, totally fine. But
in terms of new things, jackets, shirts,
shoes, bags, you likely have enough to
not consume a single thing. Same thing
with whatever is in your home. If you
got every one of those dollars back in
your life and never spent on it, where
would it take you? Now, an exercise I
really enjoyed doing this is we didn't
buy anything new for an entire year. We
made it to to be fully honest, we made
it to Black Friday. I bought something
on sale there. But in the meantime, I
said, "Hey, I can buy second hand
anything I sell something to get." So, I
sold stuff on Grailed and Depop and
local second hand, and I could buy using
those funds anything that was also
second hand. Basically consuming nothing
new. And it really changed my
relationship with the dopamine of
purchasing something when that was no
longer an option for me, or I had to
actually sell something to get there,
and the value of what I was reselling,
what do I really use, and what do I have
to make an outfit? And it was a
wonderful thing that honestly bought us
back close to $10,000 a year. Cuz you
don't think about it, but those little
things kind of add up. And $600 a month,
all of a sudden that's $7,200 a year.
Then an even bigger one was the alcohol
and going out. Now, I was at a point in
my life where I could really think about
it, where that was all of a sudden I was
like, "I'm still doing this. We're still
buying beer and wine and all this stuff.
We just had a kid. Going out and buying
any of that in my house was money that
really wasn't achieving anything for me.
It wasn't making me happier, and it was
just a suck." And I would really think
about how can you remove that from your
life? Again, for a year. And then look
at the fiscal benefit of it. Same thing
with those subscriptions. Like I
mentioned, you'll find a lot of like, "I
have this $10 thing, this $40 thing. Do
I really need it? Can I have just one of
these and alternate?" And you begin to
look at how much money you can get back
and then think about the power of what
you have to do with it. Can you save
that for your retirement? Can you save
that towards your house? Can you save it
towards starting a brand or investing in
yourself? But concentrating that for a
year, for a year being like, "I'm not
buying anything new. I'm not spending on
alcohol or vice. I'm certainly not
touching gambling, crypto, stocks. If
you're investing money, put it in the
S&P. Don't think about it." Then you're
looking at how I can constructively
using every one of these dollars and
accumulating it in my accounts with the
resources I have. And you can pretty
realistically after a month or so make a
plan of what your change looks like,
what that's going to allow you to save,
and then what you're going to be able to
do with it. And think about what that
accomplishes for your goals. If you've
never had a money year, you have an
awkward relationship with money. You
spend money to feel better. Tackling
that year is a huge thing. Which brings
us to the systems year. Every creative
is like this where even if you have a
schedule at work, you don't keep a
schedule in the rest of your life. Or
you're forgetting things or things are
slipping through the cracks. You have an
idea and don't know how to do it. So
putting a system into your life is a
crucial thing to think about. And the
system is simple enough. It is using a
calendar. It is using some sort of
system to track things. And it is having
some level of communication and
accountability around it. Let's break
those down one by one. So the first
thing you want to do is start using a
calendar. Even if you have a job where
you don't usually want or a job where
you do and it's overwhelming. What are
you doing with your time blocks? So pick
a day each week, Friday, Saturday,
Sunday, whatever it looks like. Say,
"I'm going to plan my next week." And
then you do that and you go, "Here's all
the things I have to do, my mandatory
things. I need to make sure I've done
laundry, done this at my work, gone to
whatever appointment. Here's the things
I want to do with my time." And then put
it in the blocks. How long does it take?
I'm putting it in your Google calendar,
your Apple calendar, and blocking it
off. And then really realistically
looking at what time do I have and
what's my extra time, and can I
accomplish all these things and where
are my trade-offs? But when you start
doing that every week and you actually
mark it down, this is what I'm supposed
to do it, and you stick to it cuz it's
your systems year and you're acting on
improving yourself and improving your
life, you start to see a major shift in
where you spend your time and how and if
you do it or not. And then you add on
some sort of tracking system for the
things you have to do in your life.
Apple notes or Evernote or whatever it
ends up being. Whatever's easy for you,
you have to pick one thing. Ideally with
checkboxes. There's something satisfying
and dopamine providing about checking
that box, whether it's on an app or you
have a written thing in your house that
says it's done that helps you turn into
the kind of person who loves the system.
And the third thing is communication. So
for instance, if you are at work, you
start tracking what you're working on,
what you got done that week, and sending
that to your boss. If you have a partner
in an entrepreneurial venture, you start
to track that every week. Here's what
we're working on, here's what's coming
next. Or even just for you, journaling.
Here's what I got done this week. Here's
what my plan for next week. Those three
things together with when acted on for
52 weeks straight turn you from a
chaotic person to a action person. And
this is important for a lot of people
who wonder, "There's other people my age

some people, this is innate. They're

they grew up in a way that made them

something to get excited about or apply
yourself to, now you're going to have
the kind of system in your life to help

end with the obsession year. So

want to improve how others perceive you
cuz branding is about how others think

"Oh, it's fake or you're going to turn

I was at 23 or 27. And to give you an
example, like I'm an intense person now.
I get comments about my intensity in the

bro. I loved to party. I was the center

as that life is, that's going to
accomplish the goals that they need to.
And over time, I became this person.
This person isn't fun. It's not chill.
I'm not an amazing hang unless we're
[ __ ] building something, in which
case we will drive it and take it to as
far as we can humanly go and it will be
amazing. But that took concerted effort.
And so your rebrand breaks down into a
few things. Starts with an exercise, the
archetype of who you want to be. How do
they dress? What do they say in
different scenarios? When they're
talking to someone of the opposite sex?
When they're negotiating a deal? When
they're in a scenario at work? When
they're hanging out with others? What do
they read? What do they consume? Build
that list. Start with that exercise.
List those things out and actually write
out what those actions look like. And
then it takes planning. This is why I
think this is a year. People think they
can just change their outfit and they're
going to be a different person next
month. That's the case. It takes
intention. The end of every day, going
into the next day or in the morning,
thinking about what would this person do
in the scenarios I'm going to see? In
this meeting tomorrow at work? At this
hang with my friends? With my free time?
How would this person that I want to be
present themselves? And then planning
for it. Planning the outfit, planning
the conversation, walking through that

of voice. You need the repetition of
being this new person to actually become
it. And that time, that year, needs

until you are it. And in a year, you can

unlikely. And trying to focus on

zone in on, "This is the year I become
who I plan to be. I embody their

them." And don't feel bad about it.
People will be weird about it for
whatever amount of time. You have to
pursue what you want and what you are

recommendations I have is that one of
the hardest things for people is to
change how they react to things, to
change how they talk in any scenarios.

even notice. You take a breath.
You think. If you consider your what
you're going to say before you say it,
it's almost good, almost respectable.
But you should need to have that
split-second moment of, "Am I

last year, by the way. We'll get into

sacrifice things that are in your life
to achieve a goal. This comes up a lot

something off or you're a creator and

You got to save for wherever you want to

last year for me was the year where I
was like, "Hey, I'm becoming a pretty
big creator. I made far more as a

left my job mid-year last year. I want
to say yes to all the opportunities I
like that I'm into. I want to see how
far I can push it, how much I can do.
I'm not going to have the same
friendships and relationships and casual
stuff I did. I'm I'm going to be able to
take care of myself the same way. I had
to really think about the time and
relationships with my family and how we
spend time. It doesn't mean cutting
anything off, but it means like
understanding what my responsibilities
are, what everyone's dynamics are, and
really planning for it. It's important.
And then putting my priority as it's the
work over everything. And for a lot of
people that will mean moving out of
where you're at, not hanging out with
the same people you did and don't have
the same goals, not texting back for
people that you feel bad about. Always,
every time for this year you are
choosing the work, you are choosing your
goal over everything. And if you really
do that, do not feel bad about it. Every
person you've seen get to mass success

as they do it. That's a huge thing that
is highly worth thinking about. If
you're ready for it, absolutely go. And
I will say some of the ups and downs of
this, it's going to be a long year, it's
going to be a hard year, it's probably
going to be bad for your health. That is
what it is. You don't have to choose
this year. If you have some momentum and
you want to go all in, sometimes that's
all right. And again, we're in a culture
that has us almost feel bad about it.
Don't. And also think about how you can
embrace it with people. One of the big
changes for me in my life was with my
wife and I doing okay, we're not going
to be watching like Netflix at night.
We're not going to be having movies,
we're not going to do the same kind of
hang stuff we did. And that actually
went better than expected because my
wife was like, "Hey, I want to help. I
want to be involved." And now we have
the Status Symbols newsletter that we do
together and we work on it at night. It
helped her gain some focus and priority
and decide some stuff that she wanted to
do. And again, it's not going to last
forever. At the end of the year, that's
the key thing about the obsession year,
you get to ask yourself, "Do I want this
to keep going or am I done?" This is an
important time. Give yourself that year.
Give yourself going all in for that
amount of time. But then if you're over
it, if you feel like you know what, this
wasn't worth it or I don't want to
continue, you did it. You had that year.
You locked in. It's done. You get to
really be like, "All right, you know

That's fine. You did it. Also, you can
look at it and be like, "What if I did

that take me?" And that's fine, too. But

forever. And that's the conversation

thinking about. And if you do this,
there's only one guiding light in that
year. And that light is the most
important thing is the goal. Every
single action you take is it's
supporting the goal. If it's not, don't
do it. And I put a lot of absolutes into
these years because if you have a real

or mastery or obsession, you need to do
it. You can't make an exception. You

to go a massively long way. If you

## 58. Marketing is about to change forever (and no one is noticing)

Marketing has been changing completely
and no one is talking about it. We
actually live in an era where marketing
is almost like a sport. More people know
and understand marketing than ever.
Mostly because of the internet and
content around it for the last few
years. Also, this comes with an increase
in like small business acumen because of
things like Shark Tank and all the gurus
we know and love. But a lot of the
trends that are happening are really
hard to put in the snippet. And a lot of
the people that make content about this
stuff don't really live it. And if they
do, aren't on the cutting edge. they're
too far removed or they're too
impersonal brands or selling courses or
their niche. In this video, I'm going to
walk through the actual trends and
extremely important concepts that are
not being talked about almost anywhere
on the internet. We're going to talk
about visual world building, product
marketing, 10x creative directors, how
mainstream AI ads have gone, the
clipping era, brand trips, event
rollouts, and global rollouts, the rise
of indie brands on social, and how
bigger marketing institutions are
beginning to get better now. why we need
to stay on the edge. There'll be
campaign examples for all of these. I
want this to be both fun to follow and
educational if you work in the space.
And I promise you there is no marketing
content like this on the internet. Let's
lock in. So, first I want to talk about
the app Aura and this concept of world
building. You've heard this a lot. A lot
of marketing people have co-opted this
term, but the Aura app is basically a
running app. You can showcase your run
with custom graphics and a lot of
graphics they curate themselves. What
Zach has done is using AI mostly has
taken pieces of internet culture of all
kinds and rapidly implements them into
the app. People really want to share
Aura and share this absolutely
constantly whenever they do their runs.
They make it so much more likely to
share their workouts. But each piece is
basically a piece of in internet
ephemera for lack of a better term where
Zach will grab all of these pieces of
culture from the Drake fonts to adding
like Call of Duty killreak graphics
building his own quotes and lore even
recurring characters and things they've
talked about to form the story around
his app. And what is particularly
interesting about this is this app was
basically born on X where he said he
built it in public from his initial
concept to prototypes to graphics to
beta to launch. Then he brought it over
to Instagram and then people began
sharing it and multiplying it. People
sharing this app that people have not
seen before with all these graphics and
things that look familiar and now
characters and through lines and ideas
that he's pushing through. This gives
this new visual building which is kind
of completely different than any roll
out or thing that we've seen before in
software. But it reminds me a lot of
this podcast called the technology
brothers podcast tbn if you have not
heard of this. So they rolled out with a
very strong visual feel. The podcast
hosts were in suits. They were in a more
formal environment. They were reading
tweets printed off on paper. It felt
more professional, more real, more put
together than what we'd seen prior. It
evolved into a live show that mirrors
almost exactly what would be on a major
news network. But they've now built
their brand world by basically having
snippets again just like a news site
that lives within the things like a new
profile pick for a former open AI
executive or the world's largest
longrange aircraft being delivered.
Engineers becoming a girl dad. They are
using a visual universe of the way they
present themselves, the graphics of how
they present their episodes to the
promotions that move out around it to
treat the content and the brand of their
content like a modern news network to
position against kind of more
traditional technology podcasts and
allin and antiquated stuff like that and
tell an interesting visually dynamic
story. And what's important here for all
marketers to recognize is the playbook
has very much changed about how much
media you can turn out, how constant you
can keep the conversation and the
network effects that happen when people
believe in it and have a toolkit they
can adopt. Much like we see things like
Wimbledon or the US Open or people
dropping thousands of pieces of content
are understanding fundamentally of
velocity in marketing, how much can get
covered, how much content you can put
out. TVPN is live for hours every single
day and they evolve to there from a
podcast within months and what you
should be covering and the role of what
media looks like. And I bring up this
media role because it's important for
brands too. More and more now you see
brands hiring for media roles, whether
that's coaching employees to make
content, ghostwriting for the founders,
or opening up whole media arms. A16Z is
a whole media arm coming together
because they're realizing that a lot of
lifestyle journalism and a lot of
industry coverage people want to listen
to no longer comes from journalism.
There's a stark divide between wanting
to read that the terribleness of the UX
on those sites and the unqualified
nature of most of the people that write
for those publications versus having
that happen by people that actually
operate in it with a more
straightforward relationship of a brand
gives you good content, maybe you'll
want to buy their products. you actually
know the motivations there versus
darker, more complicated motivations
that come from the traditional
journalist ecosystem. And this goes for
marketing the same way. People much
rather consume things from the marketing
creator ecosystem. People curating those
news sources because ad week or ad age
seems brutally out of touch. They can't
even make their own good content. Why
would you listen to them about what
content is? and knowing the people that
they cover most often in agencies or the
same people sponsoring these events or
that and no idea what the real economics
or implications of the relationship look
like. But along with world building, we
are in the product as marketing era. So
Skiims released this head wrap. They had
Anthony Hopkins relive his Silence of
the Lambs character as part of its
debut. And this is an example of
something that maybe they'll sell a
bunch of units. Maybe this will be a
success. I don't think so. I doubt this
will even work. but it is purely
marketing inside the product roadmap.
This has worked for a while. You would
see brands release concept products or
release products that were more meant
for virality. But now people that were
on it early, this is now built in. This
is in the product development meetings.
This is in the release schedule. They're
years into it. It's become adopted.
That's an important concept to consider
because the product development cycle
takes time. It took time to actually
activate on this as a strategy. So
someone's a couple cycles ahead. They're
already so much better at it than
others. And mainstream companies get it
too. This is Progresso. They made real
soup drops. That's purely a marketing
stone. This is purely marketing, but
they made them. They're real and people
could buy them. And this now happens in
the mainstream. For instance, this
Gustaf Westman IKEA collaboration
featured this meatball tray that again,
you may have, you're rarely going to
use, is there for virality. There's been
more conversation around this meatball
tray than the collaboration itself. And
that drives then demand for the
collaboration. That's always been the
point of these viral items is to drive
conversation about the topic to then get
people to go consume more normal stuff
cuz they don't really need a meatball
tray. But while they looked at the page,
maybe they saw something they actually
do need. This concept has gone from cool
idea to built into serious companies.
IKEA, SKIMS, Progresso is in their road
map. This is cemented. So this is
something that if you have a serious
brand should be certainly thinking about
and in the midst of. And the key thing
to solve is how do the marketing teams
and product teams work together to find
theQs, get ahead, develop the viral
item, make it both interesting enough
and functional enough they may want to
actually work for your customer,
forecast it, act on it, and make it a
recurring part of what you build
together. And this is one thing I want
to call out. You do not have a social
media team member working with your
product team. We're in the packaging
meetings. You need people that
understand how things move online
because those little changes, those
tweaks, the color schemes, the
postability of anything makes a massive

a absolute ton of value in. We'll get
there. We'll talk about LinkedIn for

bit of how the actual network works when
you're creating on there and then how
you develop a cadence that works and a
lens that works if you want to post on

start with LinkedIn on the surface. When

immediate level of cringe that none of

you. While at the same time posting on
there has a higher level of angst than
anywhere else you can post because it is
professional, because bosses are on
there, because co-workers are on there
because there's expectation. And so what
you do see is a lot of dril. You'll see
posts someone can you know announcing a

industry and then you'll have these AI

When you combine that with the

that does whatever that just makes it so
a lot of people just don't want to log

because it's actually interesting. If

brand and you are one of the admins, you
have the option to notify people at your
company that you posted. And also, if

that you mentioned that tags their

absolutely must not do. And I'll break
down some ideas to make this easy for

a cadence. I recommend that especially
for creatives and marketing people. Post
once a month is a great start or once a
week even is a great start, but get this
lower cadence. It doesn't have to be
daily. Everyone always has these like
high expectations. That's great. You
want to become a creator and influencer,
you kind of have to do that. But if you
are just trying to set up your basis on

things that happened at the brand. So
it's not like you're building some
separate identity. You're sharing what

off your role for others to see. And
you're providing some experience for
people to look into and be like, "Oh,
that's what a junior art director at
Amazon does or whatever your role is."
But establishing that cadence and
sticking to it at the end of a year,
you've now posted 12 things that show
what you do. Whenever a potential

## 61. Nike is collapsing... and it's bigger than sneakers

In this video, we're going to talk about
Nike and their awkward current state,
Adidas and their cultural comeback, and
the marketing around the World Cup. A
lot on soccer, a little bit on
basketball, but most importantly, the
state of the world as cultural titans
and smaller brands battle it out around
the sporting zeitgeist. And look, I
remember crystal clearly the moment my
older brothers showed me this, the Nike
All-Stars versus Devils ad. Nike
entering the arena into soccer marketing
in the most major of ways, right?
Creating ads nothing like we'd ever seen
before. Pure cinema, drawing the
absolute best out of the sport,
marketing at its highest level. And when
I was growing up, it seemed like they
were on a run that would be completely
unstoppable. I had branded posters
hanging in my room. When they dropped an
ad, it was a discussion point. You
assumed the great athlete would be a
Nike athlete. And this was their soccer
strategy. Sign Brazilian athletes, make
cinematic ads, and establish cultural
urgency in the United States and
globally around a sport they didn't have
deep roots in. Adidas was rooted, and
they were counter-positioning. And in
their time, they dropped incredible ad
after incredible ad. This one, World Cup
1998 Brazil at the airport, equally as
important viewing, right? In a
completely different flavor and way. All
fun and skill and expertise and stardom,
while being distinctly Nike. Simple,
fun. But perhaps my favorite ad is one
that I bring up all the time when
talking to brands now, when we talk
about cinematic content, right? You see
now, streetwear brands, athletic brands,
they're all doing real cinema. They're
all putting together lookbooks, making
their own movies, their own shows. And
this Nike first-person POV directed by
Guy Ritchie ad, that puts you in the
position of this player in this
situation, inside Arsenal, was not just
unique at the time, but is incredible
for anyone who wants to immerse
themselves inside a brand world. A truly
masterful piece of advertising. But fast
forward to today, that's not where
Nike's at. That's not what's happening
in the culture. For most people, it's
synonymous with mediocre. The shoes are
okay. You wouldn't ever buy the clothes.
Millennials have strong feelings about
the brand. They grew up with it. They
have hope for it. I have hope for it.
But, the reality of it with the consumer
is something different. And to
understand it, we should go away from
soccer and into basketball, where they
have one of the hottest athletes of the
moment. If you look at the shoes they
have, this is the shoe they have for
Victor Wembanyama right now. They're
simply bad. I had a moment with my son
when Luka was traded to the Lakers from
the Mavericks, when he was asking, "Does
he have shoes?" He's aware of the idea
of the shoes and Jordan shoes. And I
remember distinctly showing him the Luka
shoes in all the styles and being like,
"Yeah, I'm cool, bro." And I'm trying to
put that in perspective of people that
grew up in the era I did, where it would
just be like them taking an L or doing
that just seems not even comprehensible.
But, this is it. Look at the Jordan Luka
3. Like, look at this. Who is wearing
this? How is this aspirational? How does
one of the top athletes in one of the
biggest markets get represented by Nike
for something like this? And it's not
just one athlete or two athletes.
Everyone on the Jordan roster, everyone
on the Nike roster, perhaps best
exemplified by the honestly complacent
trash of the Devin Booker shoe, trying
to badly reinvent what came before it in
a really unique market with a unique
player who could have done so much more.
One of the only players with a decent
shoe and presentation, Ja Morant. But,
even then, they had to go through a sea
of whack shoe after whack shoe to get to
anything interesting and respectable.
And to the point now, when you go on to
the football cleats section of this, and
you scroll through these now, there is
very little here, if anything, that
you'd want to wear, that you'd be
excited about. And I find this
interesting that this is where this
brand is at, when we are in one of the
most interesting times in design,
possibly ever, where there is so much
talent, there is so much high quality,
and they choose again and again to put
out stuff that is mediocre at best. So,
what happened? I'm going to talk for a
bit about how jerseys began to take over
culture and how that began to actually
take share away from these big brands
used to sell you athletics. We'll talk a
bit about campaigns and relevancy in the
modern era. The brands that are taking
this share and the unique things that
they're doing. And then go into the
marketing behind the World Cup and why
this is such an important and pivotal
moment for both Nike and Adidas on the
trajectories they're going, one up and
one down. So, the cultural cache of
sportswear is at an absolute all-time
high and the soccer jersey perhaps most
exemplifies that. Brands like House of
Errors that I'm wearing right now took
that fandom that used to be expressed
through like a Nike garment and made it
simply more interesting. And that became
a major trend. It's gone all the way
down into brands like PacSun. And you'll
see lots of brands that are actually
passionate about the jersey use it
pretty extensively inside what they're
doing. KidSuper is another example I
frequently point to where the founder is
passionate about soccer and you see it
again and again as a motif inside the
clothes that they put out. But all of
this, this embracing of this used to be
traditionally focused on these major
athletic brands. But now the symbol
isn't the jersey of the player itself.
It's it as a fashion concept. And that's
an arena that Nike doesn't play in at
all. And it's not like other brands
haven't noticed. One of my favorite
stories of the last few years is Umbro's
taking a more fashion-forward approach
with the line they've done with Slam
Jam, right? And they came out and they
launched it in 2025 with this chainmail
jersey, right? Which is the perfect
example of the abstraction we have of
these artifacts we have around sports we
want to purchase, we want to remember,
we want to represent us and what can be
done with a vision around that. And when
you look at these you go, "Okay, this
creative director working on this gets
taken by Umbro but not Nike." And you
can see this brand was able to get life
breathed back into it by making really
interesting sportswear. And if you just
go through the Slam Jam collections,
there is more interesting things
happening here than across the entire
Nike site. And I say this to highlight
that it is a talent war that we are in
right now. We have more independent
talent than ever, better designers, more
creative directors, the ability to
sample and test more things, more
inspiration, more people working
together. But since the Virgil Abloh
era, none of that has landed at Nike.
And, at the same time, you have the
Fanatics world, right? The world of
cheap jerseys. My only Fanatics anecdote
is that's one of the most despised
brands by generations, right? You have I
remember at a I was at a Super Bowl
party recently, back in Feb, kids had
angry Fanatics stories, and adults had
angry Fanatics stories for they'd gotten
a bad quality or not arrived on time, or
they'd been disappointed. There's been

## 68. The 7 Levels of Brand Building

In this video, we are going to talk
about the seven stages of marketing and
brand building. This walks through
everything from what to focus on when
you're at a new brand or just starting
all the way to when you're a big brand
and trying to maintain relevance. What
are all these stages? It's really useful
to understand as a creative or as a
marketer to know where your toolkit best
applies and also to think about as a
brand owner if you're stuck or you feel
like you're concentrating on the wrong
things. So, no matter where you're at in
your journey, I think there's a lot you
can learn from this video, and I'm
excited to get into it. Let's lock in.
But first, let's talk about stage one.
What do you do at the very beginning of
your journey? What do you focus on?
We're inundated with all these
recommendations and all these different
strategies. And people often feel like
they're being pulled in so many
different directions or they need to do
something because someone says they
should do it. That's what everyone else
is doing. But here's what really is the
difference. At stage one of your
marketing, level one is one organic paid
strategy. You need to find one thing
that works. And you may test a few to
get there, but you need to be laser
focused on getting one thing that makes
money. This is the foundation from which
all the rest of your brand is made. And
honestly, nothing else matters. Not an
amazing brand kit, not awesome art
direction. It may be something different
than what you expect. And it also may
not be the most glamorous thing, but one
organic or paid strategy that makes you
money. This is important because you
need this to actually fund the next
stage of your growth. You need to
validate where your product is at. And
you need to be able to say, "Hey, I'm
making some money off this thing that
allows me to invest it further in the
business." And what you'll see happen is
a lot of businesses will start expanding
into other stuff without having a
profitable channel and they'll just get
further and further underwater. And if
you're in that scenario, which happens
to a lot of people, I just recommend
cutting back and going and focusing on
finding that one thing. And a few
examples kind of both small and large.
What I like to look at for a big brand
that I think is kind of underestimated
inside this was when David launched
their protein bars. They actually found
their channel inside Tik Tok shop, which
I don't even know if they expected, but
they had a product with a strong set of
USPS, unique selling points. Basically,
they offer the most protein to the least
amount of calories and the least amount
of sugar of any protein bar on the
market, which gives them a real leg up
to market on a network like Tik Tok
shop. And they sold a lot of bars on
commission with creators doing that that
would had far more efficacy than what
they did on social media and also help
them get lift and awareness as they went
into retail. Over in the organic world,
an example I absolutely love is Bad
Ombres. They are a frozen bean and
cheese burrito company that services
only Arizona and they have thrived on
organic Instagram where they were
continually told the story of how this
couple was starting a frozen bean and
cheese burrito company and all the
trials and tribulations that came from
that. But by focusing on carousels, on
videos, and on stories purely
organically, they've sold out every drop
again and again, knowing that that's the
key channel that helps drive their
story. But before anything else, before
you move on, before you think about
optimization or spending more money, get
to this one thing. And it may differ by
your skill set may take you some time to
find, but that is step one. Then step
two is the foundation. And if you're a
marketer and you're starting at a
company early or you're a freelancer,
you probably help a lot of companies in
this stage. That's someone who's gotten
this initial channel. They've made some
sales, they've done some things, but now
they need to move forward, build out
their team, begin to think bigger. And
so in this foundation stage, really what
you do is you lay all the foundation of
what you need to scale. You do these
things that are going to multiply the
efforts that come after it. And this is
why I don't want to ignore this stage.
You don't want to hop right to expanding
out to tons of channels. Dial in this
foundation first and it pays off
dividends. So what does this include?
This a includes landing pages. Where do
consumers land on your website and how
do they convert and how do you make sure
it's good? So there's software like
Replo that spins up multiple landing
pages and quick pages and different
experiences. And what you'll find is
that if you have different landing pages
for your different drops and your
campaigns that actually link to
specifics around them, different landing
pages for different influencers, for
different customer archetypes based on
the what you're emphasizing in the ad,
it makes a huge difference in your
overall conversion. And getting into the
habit of how do we do this? Do we do
this default in Shopify? If we are a
leadbased service, are we using
something like Clickfunnels or Lead
Pages? What are we doing there? All the
way to if you're e-commerce, you're
using something like Replo. How do you
make sure you are making the most out of
every visit you get? Related to that, we
talk about email. We talk about SMS. So,
I obviously use Omnisend for stuff like
this, but there's also pop-up software
and other things that can really help
improve it. But every time someone lands
on your site, the higher chances you get
their email, the more value that has to
you over time. And then once you have
that email or that SMS, what they're
educated with and how that works pays
off dividends as you add more and more
people to it. So, there's a software
called Aaliyah helps optimize pop-ups.
And in this stage, you're really
thinking through what am I giving people
when they sign up on my email list? What
is my offer? Am I testing discounts? Am
I putting it on every page? Am I leading
on the homepage or do I have guides?
What am I doing to make sure I get that
download? And then what technology am I
using to get it? That's one part of the
equation. Then the other part is what
does automation look like? And I have an
entire email marketing video you can
find on my profile where I dive deep
into this. But basically, how are you
educating people about your brand
automatically? How are you providing
education to new customers? The more of
those auto emails and automation flows,
that is then something that exists. If
you have a thousand more customers come
in, they automatically get into this
flow. their lifetime value increases and
it makes a huge difference. That's why
we recommend investing that time and
energy at this stage once you're making
a little money to set all this up to
then enable you to efficiently scale
after. And that leads us to the third
stage of brand building. That is the
multiplier. That's basically taking all
the things you're doing already and
putting them across as many places
before you think about something new.
So, for instance, if you're doing really

I'm already doing to get max value?" On

They kind of lean into every scalable

They put the gas on everything else cuz

velocity can keep us more relevant and
more on the internet than any brand

object and usability nature of their

objects. So, a good call out there is

from Blackpink when they were debuting

behind it using the power of art

direction principles you can learn from,

the prestige and mastige price points,
are something I've spent my entire

## 74. The Art Direction of Quiet Luxury

In this video, we are going to talk
about the art direction of quiet luxury.
How do brands that sell at the highest
price points build worlds with their
imagery and some of their choices and
especially the ones that aren't relying
on their logos or some big signatures to
do it? In this video, we're going to
talk about Lao Piana. We're going to
talk about the row. We're going to talk
about Branella Cuchinelli. We're going
to talk about Bautega Vanetta. We'll
touch on a whole bunch of things along
the way. We're going to break down the
playbook that's being used by these
brands to help justify some of their
price points visually and how to create
a world that appeals to the quiet luxury
consumer. If you're interested in diving
more into this world, I have linked
below the luxury online website report
that I did when I worked with Vang
Group, who is one of the more
prestigious luxury website builders in
the world. You can grab for free below.
I'm super excited to walk through luxury
world building with you. Let's lock in.
But first, I want to talk about the
brands I chose for this video. And then
we'll talk about the actual categories
that they use and how. So, first is Laro
Piana. Notorious for extremely expensive
clothing. I've had a moment once they
were featured heavily inside Succession.
Really capitalized on that with how many
stores they've opened and how much
they've pushed into the quiet luxury
movement in general. But a controversial
brand, not a brand I recommend people
buy. I think it is radically overpriced
for what it is. They are notedly in
trouble for exploitation inside their
supply chain, but they are the
quintessential quiet luxury brand, one
worth understanding. Then we have the
Row. The Row is one of the most
interesting ones to me because it is a
newer luxury brand founded by the Olsen

they have on a mysterious and artful
nature to actually break into this

another of the quintessential quiet

extremely high-end men's and women's

to cover it all. We're going to talk
about positioning a brand to succeed
inside of advertising. We're going to
talk about the concept of hooks and why
it actually matters in ads just as much
as content. We're going to cover
emotion, persuasion, and psychology and
review 10 psychology tactics that are
going to help you convert in your
marketing. We're going to go through
digital ad types. My friend Lucas from

through all the different types of
digital ads and keywords. Everything
from Amazon to Instagram to Google. You
get an idea of the full toolkit you have
to play with. We're going to talk a bit
about traditional advertising and its
place in the modern world, types of ads
that work, and I'm actually going to go
through a bunch of different ad types
you should be running for every business

to advertising for your business. And I

It's going to hit the money-making side

So, you probably heard the word
positioning, but what does it really
mean? Positioning starts with product is
reinforced by marketing and is about the
association your product and your brand
has inside the consumer's mind which is
a choice on your behalf and how you
create the product design the product
and how you advertise it. So a good
example is a brand like Volvo who is
associated with safety. They are
positioning themselves as a safe family
vehicle where you are basically buying
peace of mind. And the first type of
positioning that I'm going to talk about
is category creation. This is when you
create something completely new to
position yourself as a unique offering
on the market. So a good example is an
Aura ring where there had been rest
optimization and some of these other
things that existed with smartwatches or
devices but they positioned it inside of
a new form factor focused on achieving
that optimization in your life without
interfering with the rest of what's
going on. And then someone like AG1
where vegetables have been around
forever even some greens company but
they basically created a ritual around
getting what you need for your body in a
new way in a new form factor. And that
leads us really quickly to the second
type of positioning which is called
position against. This means you're
positioning against a leader or an enemy
inside of an existing category to say we
are better in a different way. I love
using that AG1 example for someone new
because there's a new company Grunts
that has come out by positioning
basically against AG1 saying you are
doing this thing that's healthy for you
but it tastes bad and it's a weird
experience. Like why are we still doing
this way? Let's give it to you the way
that you will enjoy having in a gummy.
And also that means it's not just for
adults, it's for kids. they are
positioning against an existing industry
incumbent. You look at the same thing
with you look at a local bookstores
trying to position against Amazon.
You're not buying from this
conglomerate. You're buying from your
local neighborhood bookstore. We just

classic Apple positioning itself against
PC with this kind of line of ads that
was made very famous where they're
saying this is a PC and associating it
with this like older antiquated type of
person and I'm a Mac. I'm this new
person so many more people want to be.
And another type of positioning is
really highlighting a difference in your
product through choice of words. I call
this smart positioning because it
happens so much with the word smart. So
for instance, we had phones and then we
had smartphones. So someone created that
idea of if you have this phone that's
not smart, it is therefore dumb. It was
a positioning choice. I've used this
multiple times inside my own career. One

something like this was in the defense

inside of uh the consumer drones where
basically we put chips and a set of and
apps associated with initial camera
drones and positioned as a smart drone
against dumb drones that couldn't do
some of the functionality that we had.
This is in the same way you see a brand
like eightle be like hey here's a
traditional mattress. Well it's really a
dumb mattress because we have all this
technology and all these things that
enable you to have this modern
experience. Call that smart positioning.
That leads us to value positioning. This
is positioning based on cost. This is
one of the easiest types of positions to
make where you say, I'm going to look at
what everything costs in a market and
I'm going to say how much cheaper do I
have to be for consumers to always pick
that if they are price conscious. This
is a harder and harder way to position
because it gives you less room for

but it also gives you a high likelihood
when they see it. You have a higher

around subcultures. Whether that's
around language, areas of demographics,

basically two types of content and
advertisements right now. Advertisements
are almost always what we call an
interrupt. This means that someone is
doing something else and your
advertising interrupts them versus a
choose to watch. YouTube, for instance,
is a choose to watch. When you clicked
on this video, you saw my thumbnail, you
saw my title, and you chose to watch it
versus what's next to it. It did not
interrupt your feed the way an Instagram
video when you swipe up comes up next,
then have to reel you in based on you
seeing it. Those are two extremely
different experiences. But now, if I
have an ad placement in this video,
which we probably do, it's going to

you're going into TV or you're going
into a streaming world, basically
everything is interrupt. You're
interrupting a movie or whatever it is.
And what happens there is a little
different on your phone. When you're on
a secondary device and why traditional
marketing is failing so badly is because
everyone just looks at their phone when
you're watching the NFL and they go to
that commercial, everyone just opens
their phone. When you are same thing if
you get that Amazon ad at the start of
whatever video that you're uh putting on
their streaming service, you just look
at your phone. So, not only does the ad
interrupt, but then it has to compete
with a device that is addicting in its
own way, that has better content on it.
And that's why those ads are just never
ever going to perform the way they did
10 or 15 years ago. But this is where
the hook becomes important. So, I have
whole videos where I dive in digestion
of the hooks, but really is the first 3
seconds of your video. What gets someone
to not scroll through it? Or if you're
interrupting them and no matter what it
is, how do they basically get interested
enough to watch versus like scroll away
or look at a device or click bypass as
soon as they can? And so this gives you
a extremely small window in a
combination of the first visual that
they see, the first thing that they hear
and whatever that they can read that is
on the screen to get the message across
that they need to keep going and they
need to keep watching. And you will see
this metric measured all the time and
you are meta ads manager. They have a
metric called hook rate which is the
amount of people that actually watch
beyond this. When you go into your
Instagram analytics, they're going to
show you 3 second rate is inside there.
It'll show you how many people are going
beyond 3 seconds. And you'll notice that
this is the key metric. 3 second watch
time to nonfollowers is the key metric
with which Instagram promotes your video
to people that don't already follow you
and is a huge deal. So everything that
you think about should be about 3
seconds. Now this applies in multiple
ways. It's the same thing with a
carousel or a static image. People need
to be retained on that for 3 seconds
plus for the networks to care about it
and they will judge that in the ads. And
it's also an amazing rule in more
traditional formats as well. It's like
how do I utilize you have less time when
you're looking at a banner ad, less time
when you're on a listing for Amazon. you
get a second or two seconds max. But the
same principles apply. I need to hook
in. And so an anecdote from uh the

and they're not creators. But if you're

because this gets into kind of the dark

first let's talk psychology tactics. We
have emotion, we have persuasion, and we
have culture. These are all ways that
you can kind of manipulate people into
doing what you want with marketing, for
lack of a better term. So, what I mean
by emotion, so first is storytelling.
The idea of a hero's journey, creating
compelling copy, I highly recommend if
you have any story you're trying to
tell, anything happened to your
business, you're trying to tell, founder
story, literally go to chatbt, put the
story in and say, can you rewrite this
with a hero's journey arc? You can
Google hero's journey and you will see
it's a formula that basically all the
most popular books in history tend to

go through an arc that redeems

follow a formula that works for how

stories. And then we have humor. Humor
is a super easy thing to hook with for
content, almost too easy. Why it's too
easy is because it's very hard to
finalize. One of my favorite quotes from
traditional advertising is that people
don't buy from clowns. This is a
particularly relevant quote because
that's why you see humorous creators get
a lot less brand deals rather than be a
lot less impactful when they do get them
is because at the end of the day people
will laugh. They will hook you in, but
it doesn't necessarily make you want to

technique that I'm going to talk through
is going to be more impactful than

and then reinforce with some of the
other tactics we have here, you can
begin to find a unique recipe. But
that's one of the four emotional tactics
is going to be humor. The next one is
going to be fear. And fear is the most
impactful. How do I prevent this? That
is the core idea of any fear. And you
want to tap into your consumer
psychology of I want to look bad at that
big event. I'm going to go bald before I
should. I'm never going to get a husband
or a wife. I'm going to die early.
Whatever it is. Playing those up inside
of advertising is what makes often the
most successful ads is having people
prevent the end result. And the last one
and the most intangible is aspiration.
How do you make people want to be
something better than themselves? Or how
do they basically view that buying your
product buys them into a new life? After
emotion comes persuasion. What are you
going to do within your advertisement to
actually make someone take the action
that you want? So, the first item is
social proof. Basically, showing them
through reviews or testimonials, whether
that's part of the ad or the whole
thing, the success that your product has
given them. The second one is scarcity,
saying, "This isn't going to be around
for long. This deal is going to be done
in 2 weeks. This is why the drop
marketing of fashion works so well.
You're never going to be able to get
this t-shirt again." And then we have

Huberman sells so many products as he's
developed all this authority with
somebody. And then we have belonging.
Someone buying something because they

That's emotion. That's persuasion. And

That was a whole bunch of ad types you

It's worth having in your toolkit. I'm
just going to break down some of the
rules for how I view direct response and
what I've kind of read and utilized
about it that you can look at in your
toolkit. So the first note when you
think about direct response, this is a
sales effort. This is sales focused
marketing. It's done in copy and it's
done in videos. If it doesn't produce a
measurable response, then it's not
marketing, it's art. That's like a core
of this is if it's not going for a sale,
here's how many sales I got from this
that this is not belong in direct
response. And they really focus on the
offer, not the branding. Branding
doesn't matter here. It's about the
product. Branding is a byproduct. Might
be helpful, but it's not the goal. The
offer there is front and center.
Discounts, bundles, bonuses, deadlines.
That is what makes this perform. Not
worried about vibes or looks at all. You
are focused on what you are selling and
communicating it. In direct response,
the rules always include a strong call
to action. You tell the customer exactly
what to do next. Don't just imply it.
Call now. Order today. Claim your free
gift. Ambiguity kills conversion. This
this works. And they build this out in a
structure, and that structure matters.
Headline, sub headline, offer, poof, the
offer works, call to action. It's the
most simple version. There's expanded
versions, too. And then scarcity and
urgency work. People delay actions
unless they're pushed. And you have to
in your ad basically act as the sales
rep on the phone trying to get you to
make a decision today and not call your
wife. Deadlines, limited quantities,
expiring bonuses, calling out people on
their procrastination. These are
essential tools inside of that
advertising. And then repetition,
sticking with that message, having your
we'll talk about message match in this
in the last section I do here, but this
idea that the more you say the thing,
the more you reinforce it, the better it
goes. And that repetition, especially if
you know you have a winning message, is
more important than creativity and
testing lots of messages. And then we
have positioning yourself as a trusted
expert. That's a huge part of direct
response. The person talking has to be
credible. And the more credible you are,
the easier the process goes. But Dan
Kennedy has this thing. I think it's
called magnetic marketing. Basically, it
becomes so credible and useful that
leads come to you basically pre-sold. I
look at this as Huberman selling for
AG1. Anyone is who is a fan of him has
pre-sold whatever he's going to sell
you. And that's your job as a founder or
as a person finding a spokesperson for
the brand to become that magnetic. If I
was selling you a book on marketing and
you've watched a hundred of these videos
I've given you for free, you would be
pre-sold and being like, I believe what
this guy says. I would love to get this
in book format. That's a key part of
direct response is if you're if you're
missing that, you need to engineer it or
pay for it. And then last is never be
boring. If the copy feels like a
corporate press release or a brochure,
it is dead on arrival. Entertaining,
provoking, shocking, whatever it takes
to keep them reading is the goal versus
just filling it out. If you combine
those elements together, you have this
kind of idea of direct response. It's
presented inside a VSSL or a video sales
letter where someone is literally
selling through this on a video like
this or the same thing as copy on a
lander. Get to our next phase, the last
part of this. Landing pages and funnels.
I'll touch on this briefly. It's not the
point of this video, but it matters a

that doesn't work, then basically you're
going to lose the efficacy of this thing
you made because where they're going
isn't built to convert. So, a couple
quick notes on that. Landing pages where
ads die or thrive. There's a couple key
concepts. Message match. That is the
message in your ad should match what's
on your landing page. means you need a

or to incorporate it. But if you have a
core big thing called out as your main

where they are to convert. Same thing

actually answer any of the objections

offer be scroll stopping. when you
actually have your price or your bundle
or whatever it is, having it nice and
big on the page and called out and being
like, "Wow, I need to go get that."
Whether that's the power of your
product, photography, any number of ways
you can achieve it. And last is
potentially think about a downell or an

value. But downell is important because
if you're not going to convert, making
sure you're getting email, you have some
alternative is worth thinking about if
you are seeing that you're not getting
this efficacy out of that first drop.
But getting those landing pages dialed,

## 76. The Art of Marketing (Full Masterclass)

In this video, I'm going to explain
every type of marketing. This video is
for you if you are an entrepreneur or a

the full scope of your toolkit or if you

and know how this all works. So, from
positioning to funnels to email to SEO,
I'm going to go through what each of
these is, the role it plays in the brand
ecosystem, and chat about it. By the end
of this video, you're going to have a
complete idea of the whole marketing
toolkit and how it all works together.
I'm stoked to talk through it with you.
Let's lock it in. So, for some
background, prior to becoming a
full-time creator, I was the SVP of
marketing at a brand called Gelb
Blaster. Before that, I was the head of
product and marketing at a CPG company
here in California that had several
brands. I was the VP of marketing at a
consumer electronics company called 3DR
and ran sales and marketing prior to
that, an outdoors company. So, I've been
through the ringer of marketing of all
times, from supporting retail to things
that are purely online to big brands
that are doing everything. And as part
of that, I've had to learn a bit of each
of these and think about what works and
what doesn't in different types of
businesses. And there isn't a lot of
education around this kind of modern
marketing. So, if you do want to learn
about it, just going to walk through
each piece and what it is, some
examples, and see what we can build. And
I'm going to start with the most
important one, the most important thing
that I feel like every business needs to
consider and every marketer needs to
have a grasp on and that is positioning.
So what is positioning? Positioning is
how you take your product to market and
how you compare it or put it in the
market compared to your competitors.
There are a couple different types of
positioning. So for instance, when you
walk into a Best Buy, you will see
shelves that have three types of
products. Best Buy merchandises their
products with a good, better, best
strategy. So, they will have a best,
like a best-in-class item. Say if you
were in action cameras, it might be a
GoPro. And that's going to be expensive.
That's going to be sold on brand or on
really high-end stuff. And you're going
to have a better, the core category
where they make the majority of the
sales, like your average product. And
you're going to have a good, which is a
product that positions itself basically
purely on price that isn't as good as
everything else. So in headphones, you
might have a Bose or a Beats at the top,
more in the middle company, and then
something on the cheaper end, say a
Skull Candy or someone like that who
tries to present a premium experience,
but does it at a lower price point. But
positioning is more than price. It's
about who is your product appealing to
and how and how does it differentiate
from the market. And it's a moving
target because your positioning depends
on the other offerings that are
happening around it. So if you sell
computers, then other people are selling
computers with similar specs. Are you
positioning it as a gaming PC or as a
thing for work? It's about thinking
through what the angles are for your
product. And I like to look at an
exercise I think every brand owner and
marketer should be able to do for the
project they work on as a comparison
between yourself and any other products.
So if you are in an elevator an elevator
pitch scenario and you say oh I work in
the headphones industry or I have a
fashion brand etc. and someone says oh
like this brand and they call out one of
the big brands in the space. You need to
be able to respond and say oh we
actually do this. Here's our difference.
Here's how we compare. here's you would
why you would buy our product and that
is going to be your kind of positioning
statement and that quick synopsis is
going to be kind of a big driving force
in your product and so a few things I
want to call out as tactics for this
that are worth considering so obviously
there is price you can always look at a
market that has some success and say
we're going to do something cheaper as
an easy positioning strategy on the far
end a much harder positioning strategies
you can always charge more by having a
lifestyle product something that people
aspire to and want that has that brand
technique but that is going to be
something that has a lot of intangibles
that are hard to describe within
companies and hard to achieve with
marketing. There's also a few other
techniques. One of my favorites is
called position against. This is when
you take a big item or a popular item
and you pick something bad about it and
you say we are not this. Our main
characteristic is that we are not the
enemy here. I like to think about the
whole prebiotic soda category, the
lollipops and poppies. Their main
position is we are not Coke. We are not
bad for you. We are the same thing just
not evil. And there are a lot of
companies that present that in a certain
way. Similar to this, but not exactly
the same, is what I'll call
counterculture positioning. Liquid death
is a perfect example of this. Deathwish
coffee and black rifle coffee are a
perfect example of this where you say
the mainstream of culture consumers
inside a category, whatever it is, do a
thing. They have a certain lifestyle.
They have certain things they like.
We're going to be the exact opposite of
this. And usually you will see it is
synonymous with a more gothic look, a
more extreme look. And there's room for
kind of an extreme vertical in basically
every category saying we're going to
take all the things around the branding,
even around the product, and max it out.
I like to bring up DeathWish coffee a
lot because basically they said we're
going to have more caffeine than any
other coffee. We're going to put a skull
and crossbones on the logo. It's not
going to be some fun picture of a
Colombian farm or this these beans were
grown. It is going to be like a skull
and crossbones. That is a good example
of like a counterculture positioning.
But enough about how you actually put
your brand out there in the universe.
Let's talk about some of the other
factors. So, the next thing I want to

You may have heard this term before, but
really what a funnel is is your web

combining websites and where people land
and engage with your product and email.
And I will hit those one at a time.
Email being a separate section. So, in
your marketing, you need to consider the

considered a funnel is because people
drop off along it. You start at the top.
All this traffic comes in. They see you
on social media. They watch your videos.
they see your ads, whatever it is, that
is your top of funnel. And then people
get to your website, your funnel is cut
down a little bit more. And then from
there, you get people that explore more.
They go to the product page, funnel gets
cut down more, add to your cart, finally
check out, customer. You have basically
seen your funnel shrink until it gets to
the very end. And what good marketers do
is they analyze every part of this
funnel in two ways. The first is what
are they doing from a marketing
perspective at each level? What do you
do to generate brand awareness with big
campaigns or influencers or ads? What do
you do once you actually get that

zone in on here. When a customer comes
to your website, what is their
experience? How do you basically lose as

as many people to actually purchase with
the highest value by the end of their

important one for every marketing team
to think through because if you are

that soft call to actions help enable

people to sign up, getting them to take
step one gets you closer to step two and

only option is to buy. And so I always

opt-in areas. Now, when we really talk

secondary call to action. So in that
example I had of, hey, we want people to

section on every page, get your email or

this, you know, seven video bundle to
show you exactly like how to optimize
your fitness journey or whatever it is.
Give them free content, give them like
some value discount code, whatever it
is, you're going to get a higher rate.
And so your optimization as a marketer
is going through all those decisions and

How do I put them on page on your

There's your email campaigns and your

the better that journey is, much like

so a core thing to think about,

I'll talk about throughout this that is
what can you answer for the customer

thing because a lot of brands will have
a starter product. So, for instance,

of products. Even if you buy a car, if

how your product is presented there. You
will notice things like products that
have little tearback boxes like protein
bars you can stick on a shelf that have
some branding on them. That's like
entry- level merchandising. You'll see
little tags are called aisle
interruptors. When you're walking down a
retail aisle that someone has placed
there that promote a product that call
out that you should go look at it. These
things work. Same thing with displays
you may have. You go into an Ulta or
Sephora and you look at how crazy the
display is for a different brand. they
are getting people to consider a brand
because retail psychology is basically
if someone actually takes the time to
look and consider and look at your
product you have a high chance that they
are going to buy it is about getting
that first look and if you get that
first look it is your job of your
product to lose that you have the
opportunity to lose it most of the time
you're going to be able to win it so you
need to be thinking about not only how
do you support traffic and interest at
the store level but then once they're in
the store how do you make sure you are
called out as an option and then you're
going to get to the last part which is
retail training some retails have
trained sales floors some don't if they
do like the outdoor stores industry, REI
and Dick Sporting Goods and stuff. Train
those people as much as they can. The
more training they have, the more
incentive those employees have, the
better they are going to be able to sell
your products. And a big part of what I
did when I worked as a marketer in
retail support was that training. Online
programs, inerson programs, field teams
to basically make sure every person who
was selling that product had it in their
hands, had experienced it, would
recommend it, and as much education on
all the key questions. Now, we're going
to get to the fun part, content,
influencers, and then we'll end on
events and partnerships, and you'll have
the complete picture of what modern
marketing looks like. So, content,
everything now is becoming content. What
do I mean by that? I mean that used to
think of content as just what you put on
your social media page. But all the ads
you run on meta, that's content. Ads you
run on your out of home or on TV, that's
content. The training that you're going
to end up doing for those retail
employees I mentioned is content. Events
and partnerships you do, even if someone
shows up to a physical thing, guess
what? They're making content. They're
making videos about it. just sending
pictures to their friends. Same thing
with your packaging experience. Almost
all roads lead to content. And so it is
a critical thing to be thinking through
from your business perspective. And so
this goes from actually testing your
videos to designed assets, etc. And so
what I'll say here, I have lots of other
videos around content as a whole, is
your job as a marketer to be as in-depth
on this as possible. The most useful
marketers understand social media
networks. They know to tag related
videos for shorts on YouTube for a
longer length. They know how to label a
tutorial as something that's searchable
when you actually put it on YouTube.
They understand that Tik Tok brand
accounts are not going to perform as
well as creator accounts. They should
probably have a multi-account strategy.
They're gonna know how to use trial
reel. It's your job as a marketing
leader not just to rely on your social
media person, but to have at least one
person, including you, in the trenches
knowing how this works and thinking like
a creator as to what should succeed.
Then shifting over into influencer
marketing, which is nice and tangential.
So there are two key types of influencer
marketing to understand. The first is
seeding and the second is paid
influencer marketing. So seeding is
sending out your product for free to
people to post. This is still a
fantastic technique in this year of our
lord 2025. I would be sending out as
much stuff as you can afford to
especially if you have a cheaper
product. And this involves building a
system to find influencers that are
useful or people that are up and coming.
Contact them friendly actually send them
things. check and monitor that someone
actually posted this or not and begin to
build a system where you graduate some
of those people into doing paid work and
where you kind of monitor what gets
posted or not and make sure your product
is getting out there in the world. That
is kind of initiative one. Initiative
two is finding people you can pay to
promote your product that generate sales
and interest that is worth their amount
of dollars. And the key thing I want to
iterate there is I really feel like
long-term partnerships, doing multiple
posts with influencers, building out
longer things versus one post for x
amount of dollars is the way to think
about a lot of this. Now, what you as a
marketer need to have is a way to track
this like a CRM system, a way to monitor
this, and a way to find people. And
identifying those three things is a
complicated step and a difference for a
lot of businesses between success and
not. And the more you outsource that to
agencies, which there are plenty of good
ones, the more it is costing you, right?
There's going to be an upcharge on all
that, the harder it is to justify the
value. And the closer you have it to the
chest, the more useful it is. And as a
marketer, there are so many marketers I
see hired now because they say, "Hey,
you know, I have a 2,000 person seating
list. I have the addresses for all of
them. I know all of them open the
packages and I can do that day one when
I come in your company. That is a hard
thing to turn down when you are hiring.
So worth considering. And you go
business to business, collection to
collection with that same one too. All
right. So last topics. We're going to
talk about events. We're going to talk
about partnerships. So a few different
types of events. There's consumerf
facing events where you basically have
your consumers come and experience
something. There's B2B events where you
are putting your product in front of
retailers and trying to sell to them.
And there's selling events. So let's
break it down. So selling events first.
I actually really encourage brands to do
selling events. So for instance for my
valuables brand I went to Complex Con.
Complex Con is a selling event. Kids
come to buy clothes there. I sold a lot
of clothes there. We came about break
even on attending that show. That is a
very big net positive to generate
awareness, generate enough money to
justify going there. But also you'll see
things like whether it's a farmers
market or a car show or whatever it is.
There are probably places where your
audience gets together that it'd be
great to build a repeatable format with
which you can generate money at because
selling in real life is still an amazing
thing if you have a good extroverted
person versus purely selling online.
Those are consumer shows like that. I
would highly consider what that looks
like. Then you have B2B shows are more
expensive. These are shows like the
Magic Convention in Fashion where you
can actually go there and have a booth
and retail buyers come. Same thing for a
lot of these shows like even like EPA in
Berlin, consumer electronics buyers come
there to buy for Europe. There's all
kinds of shows to have like buying,
selling. Now Expo West is a CPG show for
food and natural organic products that
buyers come to to find brands to carry
on their shelves. So a couple things
worth noting for that is to succeed at
shows like that, they cost more money
and you have to take it into your own
hands. You need to be scheduling
meetings with buyers, filling it up,
following up, and actively selling at
those shows. And for a lot of people,
it's a good way to get in front of
people, but you need to have your ducks
in a row. Understand your distribution,
understand where you can send product,
know how to get meetings with people, be
reaching out on LinkedIn, reaching out
on cold email, trying to get people by
your booth and attracting it. And have a
goal, whether it's for a consumer show
or a B2B show of it cost us this much
money, we need to write this much in
POS, or we need to get this much in
direct sales. Because a lot of times
people will begin to do these events,
they won't have that figured out and
they'll start to realize there is not
real brand ROI in doing this if you are
not trying to get to a number. And that
ties to the first thing I mentioned,
which is these more consumerf facing
events to basically appreciate customers
and do interesting things. And in the
social media era, if one of those takes
off and people make a lot of content,
it's kind of more value to these than
ever. But this is a slippery slope where
if you do do those kind of events, you
need to basically activate your
customer, make sure they have a great
experience, make sure it's social media
friendly, and use it to create your own
content. you're going to have a very
hard time justifying ROI. So last, we're
going to hit on partnerships,
influencers, and collaborations, and you
are going to have a complete picture of
kind of modern marketing. So in
partnerships, this is what you are doing
with others. Basically, 1 plus 1 equals
2.5 to push your brand out there. So a
couple things we can talk about there.
The first is collaborations, like we've
all seen, people coming together to
create a product. Second is licensing.
It's a partnership type where you
basically say, "Hey, I'm going to pay a
royalty on a product to be able to sell
a version of it." I'll talk about
licensing first. So, you may have seen
the brand Warren Lotus, popular fashion
brand. He licenses the NBA and the NFL,
makes versions of his clothing for every
single team. He pays a fee. He pays a
percentage, and you'll see they'll have
like little logos and things on on some
of them, and he's able to use this
really popular iconography to sell to an
existing fan base. This is a genius
marketing thing for him, but he's able
to take his his iconography he's
established, and sell a thing to a
passionate fan base who is more likely
to say, "Oh, I don't want to support
that. I want to support that and the
Celtics or and the Cowboys at the same
time." And this is something everyone
from Simple Modern to Represent, there's
all kinds of brands that do this. And
that is a paid partnership. A paid
partnership should 100% be on the table
for your brand when you have the volume
to support it. And then collaborations
is really about a structure. And I'll do
a whole collaborations video. I want to
bring one of my friends on who's a
specialist in this at one point, but
this is about how can you create a
product and then co-arket it together.
And usually where this comes down to is
logistics. Who's paying for the product?
How much does each person get? Who's
selling it? Who's responsible for what
assets? like you really breaking down
responsibility and agreeing to it and
then having something come out. But this
is just another good example I think a
lot of brands should be doing as long as
you can create something interesting and
unique. And the best way to think about
this as a marketer is a collaboration
schedule where you go, we have all of
our normal releases. We have our spring
release and our fall release. We do new
product once a year. You know, if
there's big spaces in there, can we do a
collaboration that fits that's a new
product to fill that space? Is a great
entry- level way to look at it without
having do overkill. Another way to look
at is a whole program the way Monontlair
does with Genius where you may say,
"Hey, we actually want to do a a a
branded program over time where a bunch
of people fill these slots." This is
great for a bigger brand to work with
smaller brands. Uh those are just
different ways to think about it. So
anyway, I know we covered a lot of
ground inside of this. If you like this
kind of conversation around marketing
and brand strategy, my newsletter called
Hyper is at
mail.hyperstudios. I'll link it down
here. I talk about stuff like this every
week. I didn't hit on some like kind of
a little more nuance stuff like PR and
communications things that kind of don't
matter as much. And there's a whole
chunk of this that is performance
marketing and spending money on ads that
is probably worthy of its own video.
Another one I'll try to bring an expert
in on. But just defining all these
categories and just thinking through
what they can do for your brand, I feel
like is an important thing to learn. And
thanks for bearing with me.
