have to overthink some amazingly deep
creative strategy. Same thing with the
product shots when they do show just
product without some of that same
context. They all have this like lightly
surreal nature to it in terms of like
those don't actually stand like this.
What's happening in this corner? The way
these are kind of drifting or flying,
the fact that these are on the clothes
pins, even their basic shots, all just
have a little bit of intention. I call
it simple surreal. But I also want to
call out the celebrity face. So Haley is
obviously the founder of this and she is
all over their ads and content. And if
you are an influencer brand, even a
small one or a celebrity brand, you
should be using that. When you go to my
valuables page, I am all over it. Not
the only model. probably want to be the
only person telling the story, but a lot
of brands feel like they need to step
back from some of those founders and
integrators. And guess what? If it

other things they do, like putting the
juice boxes as an item that they have
inside their events or using the scale
and the packaging as a design for an
interior. But this is basically a
creative direction toolkit visually for
a brand. And what I would encourage you
to do if you're looking for how do you
build that world yourself are just to
break this into a couple key things you
can use as a playbook. What objects do
you associate with your brand? So for
instance, you say I want to do food, but
I want to do luxury objects or I want to

brand? And then there's the people and
what are the people doing? It's like who
are the folks? Is it always models? Is
it always everyday people? Are they
certain racial makeup or age makeup? But
then what are they doing? Cuz they're
almost always having a night out inside
the road. They are going into a
lifestyle of a road girl. You need to
think about what that lifestyle looks
like for your people. That's an exercise

when you do product shots, what do you
do for product shots and not have them

look you can do that makes it stand out
in any way, shape or form? Yeah, we
always do concrete product shots, but we
have animals on them or whatever it is.
What is one thing that makes it stand
out? And then think about application.
How do you actually show that inside
your website, your email, your events?
So, I like this uh models night out
example perfectly because they said,
"Hey, we are going to do this. We're

we're integrating it into our email."
And maybe that's vignettes on some sort

And if you want to go one level deeper,

do you do that same thing? Right? And
I'm going to pop into a subboard here

They're shot in this context with

country and that's happened through the

strategically through things like being
the Olympics partner for this for so
many years and many perceive the brand
as prep but the real story is that his
work is cinema.
>> It was perfect
perfect everything down to the last

where it is. But this came together in a
different world in a different time.
We're going to bridge into the Japanese
brand copy. Talk about what you can do,
what you can actually learn from a brand
like Ralph Lauren and apply yourself.
And I think there is almost no better

you.
There are so many lessons in how to

you are going to love this video. I

nowadays everyone glorifies selling a

the machine of the world around you. But

we'll have a little fun along the way.
Come for a ride. Let's lock in. I want

## 99. The internet is splintering (2026 social trends playbook)

So, in this video, we are going to talk
about the new rules of the internet, the
internet codes, if you will. And
basically break down what is happening
in social media, internet culture, and
the zeitgeist in general. So,
specifically, if you are a creative, you
work in marketing, you have a brand, or
you're just interested, and you've heard
a lot of these terms, you can think
about their implications for culture,

you get what's called a color card. So,
for example, if you like a particular
fabric, they're going to show you some
of the variations, washes, and just
colors that are available in that fabric
and its detail. This is what you're
going to pick from and be able to
actually give to your factory. Your
factory will call them up, order the
amount needed to make a sample, and then
eventually order the full amount.
they'll be negotiating with them through
the cost per meter and the kind of
minimum quantities that they need to
actually purchase fabric. So to actually
make the most of coming to a place like
this, you need a manufacturer who's
going to be a conduit between the things
that you find and what you actually want
to make. And so APM that we were just
in, they make some of the material for
some of the valuable shirts that I've
done and are just overall like an
incredible provider for 100% cotton and
for anything you want to do for sweats
or for shirts. You'll really notice when
you go get material from someone like
them versus when you get it from just a
vendor the difference in the quality and
how much a difference that makes even in
an item as simple as a t-shirt. So it's
awesome that we had so many people from
all over the world, Australia, Thailand,
United States, Europe actually come with
us and be able to experience that. It
was a super fun time and shows a lot of
the magic of a city like this. And that
is my itinerary. If you want to come to
here, I highly recommend you go to runi
which is the tag and trim store. Go to
Zangda which is the big market. I would
be wandering around this area where Utah
and a few of these other places are.
They have more premium fabric. YKK
zipper store and trim stores here. Just
go around and find anything you can and
you can really get into that fashion
experience. So, why would you actually
do that? Well, fabric is what makes some
of these brands stand out. It's what
makes your stuff better than others. And
you'll have suppliers that will show you
some of the options they have, but it's
rarely like the best option. So, if you
want to make things that stand out for
your brand, you want to go find the best
fabrics, hunt those down, bring them to
your manufacturer, be building that
lineup, and this is the place to come
for that. And along with that comes all
the inspiration. You walk into stores
like Runie, you get to see what modern
brands are doing for their tags and
trims, find something that might spark
an idea for you, and it is an amazing

## 105. What ACTUALLY Makes People Buy Things (Pricing Psychology Explained)

In this video, we're going to talk about
the psychology of why people buy. Every
single day, whether you shop online or
shop in real life, you are inundated
with tricks from $9.99 as a dollar value

## 106. What exactly is the ＂taste economy＂？

In this video, we are going to talk
about a term that I hate, but that is
very important. The taste economy. Say,
what does this even mean? What are the
factors that led to it existing? And
what does it mean for commerce? Both how
we buy and how you market and sell and
brand your own products. We're going to
talk about the educated consumer. How
consumers now have way more knowledge of
everything in the world than ever. And
then how you actually consume based on
that. Then we're going to talk about the
archetypes of the premium buyer. Who
overpays for luxury today? Why do they
do it? and how do you understand them?
If you are in marketing and branding,
make sure to stay to the end because I
have some specifics and some tactics
there from my experience selling premium
items. But the goal of this video is to
have you think about why we consume what
we consume, the factors that are at play
on social media, in media in general,
and in brand building that make us buy,
how luxury actually works, why our
brains think the way they do, and how
this changes the world around us. Let's
lock in. So, I'm going to start with an
analogy from food, a flavor analogy.
>> Welcome to flavor time. You may have
noticed in recent life everything has
started to taste amazing. Raising Canes,
Sweet Green, Goop Kitchen. There's a new
generation of food that's basically
engineered so that our taste buds just

Dorito as a kid. You never had a chip
that tasted that intense before. And all
of a sudden, this feeling came over you.
You're like, "Why have I never had
anything that tasted this good?" We had
those hyper strong flavors, at least in
my childhood. Mountain Dew, Doritos,
like the high sugar candy before this
whole natural food movement. you would
just be shocked by the efficacy of
flavor of this. But now we have all this
really refined flavor all around us. Way
more global flavors, all these hot
sauces, everything in the world. Why do
I bring this analogy up? Because in that
world where now if you want to have like
a piece of white bread or like a single
vegetable, it feels like life with the
volume off because everything around it
has become so amazing. This is why so
many people will get an Uber Eats now or
do the easy thing because it's also that
much better. And this ties into the
nonliteral taste economy. And let's
start with what does the taste economy
even mean? So we used to buy what we
need. I might need a new jacket once
every couple years. I you buy whatever
hand soap is at the grocery store. If
you really wanted a candle, you get
something normal. If you wanted a car,
you got your Corolla. But a paradigm
shift happened and it happened for a
couple reasons. The first being the
democratization of supply chain. So
Alibaba in China, the whole concept
behind Alibaba was that all these
factories used to have to go to China to
find an app now gave you access to any
person in the world could look online
and see what someone made and get it.
And the same exact thing happened
whether it was from import Yeti or
global trade shows, Thomas Net, whatever
it is, all of a sudden anything you want
to make anywhere in the world from
Turkey to the Middle East to Italy to
Portugal to the US, more people can make
than ever. So more and more brands
popped up. And at first they started to
compete on price. You would see all
these Chinese brands pop up on Amazon
with just cheaper versions of what was
successful. So there became a whole
economy around cheap. But for people
that still wanted something good, all of
a sudden the competition stepped up. The
nice thing all of a sudden became
cheaper as well. Things that used to be
a luxury that people could upcharge for
and they had to go hunt or was only made
in Italy. All of a sudden was being
duped all around the world at extremely
similar quality. So the nice thing
became not that much more. And that's
factor one. Factor two, you used to have
to dig to care. If you wanted to know
what an amazing fragrance was, you had
to go research. You had to go hunt it
down. Now with the internet, with
Instagram, with Tik Tok, you are
presented so much more information about
your options. Far more people in the age
of 14 to 16 know what a gourmand is than
ever before. People understand
materials. They know of brands that they
wouldn't have heard of any other way. It
is given to us, shown to us in how we
consume. On social media, we are
presented with brands, luxury
experiences, and high-end items in our
feed right next to everything else we
would never have seen on a TV ad in the
early 2000s. So, caring has become easy.
And those two factors of the nice thing
not being that much more expensive
because of competition. And the fact
that nice things we never would have
known are being shown to us has made it
easy to care, has made it easy to have
taste, to see apartments that look nicer

I could do that or I could buy that."
And you know what? That's not that much
more expensive than what I was going to

of happiness. All this education, all

gives us identity. And there's an
economy around it where people are
participating in buying things they

traditional things than what they spent
prior. And the industry is so massive.
There's so much dollars that have come

like the taste economy. Now we're going

the new economy to sell them. So

You know this. You are on YouTube. You

do you in your mind want the softest

you, and every customer interaction,

The numbers from launch have customers

Commerce becomes table stakes. You can

And now over the last few years, I've

I'm sure the luxury brands wouldn't love

We now have this class of educated
people in the taste economy who

millennial taste demographic. Then we

This is the person who reads like a man

They are overspending in the name of

not seen a Gucci AI ad campaign. And if

Whatever it is, whether they have the

zone in if you sell a premium good on

able to reach online with content. And
I'll end this section before we go into
this kind of new economy and how it

discussing sociology and psychology for
a second. So, if you've been watching my
short form content and you've followed
me for any amount of time, if you're new
to this, I'll explain a bit my strategy
here. You'll notice it's shifted. I talk
a lot now about insights. I will notice
something happening in culture and I
will create a position around it. I will
take a stance. I will say, I see this
insight. People are doing a certain
thing. They are buying a certain type of
home. Men and women are interacting with
each other differently than they did 10
years ago. Brands are leaning into the
fact that people are increasingly
lonely. Whatever it is, I will take a
stance. I will say I believe a certain
thing. Here's my thesis. And you'll see
in the comments, people either agree
with it or don't agree with it. And this
is because I'm sharpening my blade for
basically how marketing now works
because there's two types of sociology
psychology insights here. And this is
really important to understand. One is
there's insights people know about
themselves. They say, "Yes, I bought
this thing because I want to make
friends. I'm lonely." But you have to
realize there's a conflict in inside
everybody inside their self where
they'll be like, "Oh, I don't want to
believe that about myself. So of course
I'm going to say it's wrong. Of course,
I like my house at the size that it
actually is even if it's too big or too
small for me." I've convinced myself. We
do a lot of convincing of ourselves as
human beings. It's completely natural.
But your job as someone in marketing or
in sales or in social media or
developing a product, working on any
brand is you need to begin to decipher
this. You need to go, is this a real
insight or is this what someone says
about themselves and develop conviction
in your stances and take opinions that
you can then back up with content or
advertising. And I've noticed that more
and more, um, I used to be a very
operational marketer. I can set up a
certain number of ads. We can do a
certain amount of content. We will test
different messages and landing pages and
we will hammer our way to making money
online or supporting our retail partners
through those decisions and messages we
test. Now, I've noticed more and more of
this has become commoditized, right?
Making the landing pages, doing the
media buying, creating the content.
There are more and more and more people
that can do that. It would never be
worth my time to dollar ratio to do any
of that work. The only thing that's left
that's important is the insight is the
idea that can be outperform all these
different things that people can do. It
can make a creator make great content.
It can get a person to convert from a
landing page. It gets someone actually
pay attention to an ad. And so I'm
hyperfocused now on what all those ideas
are and building basically a compendium
of them. And that is a important way if
you are in this world at all or you care
how people think to catalog the things
that are happening to you in your life.
And so I will end this last point. This
is very focused on marketing and brand
people. What is this new economy like to
sell to this premium buyer, this taste
economy? Cuz it's completely different
than the previous world. So marketing
used to work like you would have your
own social media accounts. You have your
Meta account, your Tik Tok account, and
you would have paid ads. You'd run ads
on Meta. You could run ads on banner
ads. You could put billboards up, buy
things in magazines, your newspapers,
and then maybe you would have like some
celebrity endorsement or a few

the marketing stack. The last time I had
an SVP of marketing job was basically at

being formed. And we were participating
in this new world I'm about to describe
where it's like you have your own
account. You are doing paid media to
promote those owned accounts. But then
you have in what we call enabled
account. People that you seed product
with for free. People that are creating.
You may have 20, 30 creators, hundreds
of creators that are making content
about your message. You have influencers
that you're paying for campaigns. You're
doing experiences or events, but you're
doing it to help enable people to create
content. When you put the billboard up
in Times Square, it's not just about
having people see. It's about having
people take a photo of it and share it.
It's the enabled economy. And this goes
side by side with the taste economy. we
talked about the in the beginning where
taste be comes from this multiplying
factor. It can't just come from your
brand has to come from the world of
people talking about things. And then
the multiplier of that is you can take
any of those things you can put money
behind it. You can boost it. You can
whitelist the ads. You can push that
message out further using your dollars.
So your organic plus you're enabled plus
dollars gives you this much bigger piece
of the pie for marketing. And on top of
that it leads to a more organic
conversation. You execute all of those
well there's even more conversation
around your brand going out in the
world. And a great example from this is

at Coachella. They are making videos

## 107. What japan understands about branding no else does

Everyone you know has been brainwashed
into wanting to go to Japan. Do you
notice a massive influx on your feed of
baggy pants girls and selvage denim guys
roaming the streets of Tokyo? You're not
alone. The yen has depreciated 44%
against the US dollar since 2019, making
Japan one of the best value destinations
on Earth. In 2024, they welcomed a
record high 36 million visitors, 53%
over the prior year, and their goals
were actually set much higher. But in
one of the most interesting signals,
typically in tourism, visitors prefer
experiences, but Japan's become a
shopping destination first. People are
going to buy things. And in this video,
we're going to explore that arc. Cuz if
you want to learn about brand, you want
to learn about quality, but all of the
dynamics happening on a global scale
around marketing, there are so many fun
and interesting topics. So, I'm fresh
back after a week and change out in
Tokyo with my family, seeing and
observing a lot of this in person,
participating in the shopping economy.
And in this video, we're going to talk
about everything from the creative
directors of convenience stores, their
department store culture, the
opportunities in branding and
duplicates, the oddball scenarios around
licensing that are playing out globally.
Let's lock in. In Japan, they don't just
consume brands, they obsess over them.
And the consumer experience is treated
like real hospitality. And there's no
place to better call this out and start
this video than with convenience store
culture. You will see again and again on
your feed there are viral 7-Eleven Japan
videos. You can get an egg salad
sandwich. I went there with my son, he
can pick out a bowl of frozen fruit and
then make a smoothie right there in a
7-Eleven for like $2.50. But 7-Eleven
and their culture there isn't the story
as much as Family Mart. One of the big

primary convenience stores. You have
Lawsons, you have Family Mart, and you
have 7-Eleven. But why Family Mart
stands out is they actually announced
NIGO as their creative director last
year. If you're not familiar with NIGO,
he founded A Bathing Ape, the BAPE

Nowhere with one of the founders of
Undercover. The name is an idiom for
overindulgence and complacency. It
almost poked fun at itself and Japanese
youth living off their parents' wealth.
So, there's an interesting kind of
streetwear dynamic and culturism from
its start. And a huge component of the
scarcity model. This initially started
partly by accident. The brand had a
limited budget when they dropped 50
t-shirts, it's all they had. And they
famously really worked this ever since,
including canceling wholesale outlets,
focusing on their own flagship stores,
and being a great global representation
of hypebeast culture overall. And they
continue style. Their Kids Super collab
this year was hot and in demand 30-plus
years later. Nigo has taken that across

the most prolific position he's had,
which has been at the artistic director

called convenience wear. Where they have

well-priced, lightly branded items that

the other store with everything else
that's in the aisles and your snacks and

Helvetica laid out, very well-priced
product. But in this competitive world
that they're in, where there's some

Major brands they're competing with. And
there's all this tourism culture, where
people are choosing to much I want to
experience extra wide." People make
their lists
from the internet. All of a sudden, this

interesting. And so, with someone like

then, second is how does that product
experience become more than just Family
Mart the convenience store, but
convenience wear and these convenience
items become their own brand, their own
tool to be able to attract attention and
to be able to get customers to spend
money in different ways. And these two
actually merge together. So, I visited a
concept store they had in basically a
high-end office building where it was
just a convenience wear showcase where
they had joggers, umbrellas, socks,
towels, pants conveniently laid out. You
just grab them, scan, and go with
nice-looking stanchions, interesting
merchandising. It's basically an
extension of your average convenience
store, but played out in an
untraditional environment. And begins to
show you a bit of how the brand is
thinking and where they're going. I
think this is interesting. I think this
is ahead of what's happening in Western
culture where you've seen things like
high-end specialty groceries, you've
seen the Erewhons of the world focus on
collaboration, bring in things like
merchandise and drops to make it into an
experience and a tourism destination in
LA. But that's operating at the highest
end, at the luxury end. That's a given.
That's going to happen. Well, how do you
engineer this in the rest of the world?
How do you differentiate when everything
becomes commoditized? And this is part
of a bigger topic, right? And one that I
feel like Japan is most close to. Is
that everything can be copied so easily
now. You've seen my videos about this.
You can go on Import Yeti and you can
look up any brand and see what factory
they're importing from. That goes for
beauty, that goes for apparel. You can
hack your way down to who someone's
supplier is. You can go to China and
find them yourself. You can duplicate
almost any good. The gates have been
broken to be able to make almost
anything you want and in quantities
lower than ever. And so, if you decide
to make something niche or marketed or
presented in a different way, even if
it's successful and mainstream,
you're able to take some of that market.
We see the same thing in software now
with people being able to just quickly
replicate things with AI coding. But
Japan has always seen that directly
because China's right next door, right?
There's still a barrier to entry that
you had the US pre-Alibaba, pre-the
internet and TikTok culture where you
actually have to go hunt a bunch of that
down. That was happening in Japan
constantly. Anything nice would
potentially be bootlegged by China and
easily imported. And so, there's just a
proliferation of extremely well-made
things, extremely cheap things all
throughout. And so, in this lowest end
culture and the things you buy most
quickly and as a convenience the
branding and competition of that is
ahead of where you consider something
like that in the US. And the creative
director is one of the last tools that
people have, right? If you have the
equal ability to buy anything, I can go
to any grocery store, I can go to any
convenience store. There's a Quick Trip
next to a 7-Eleven next to a Casey's.
How do you pick what you're going to do?
Does one of them represent your values
better? Does one of them have a more
interesting experience you can pick up?
Does one of them have an item that
they're known for? When everything is
the same, the identity becomes more
important than ever. So thinking about
how in any normal business can you
differentiate yourself through what a
creative director would do? How could
they design the experience to be more
shareable and more compelling and more
interesting than the surroundings? That
might take time. May want to not run
that right away. Whenever you're doing
your redesign, can you begin to bring
that creativity in? Can you begin to
collaborate and have exclusives that
people want to show up and buy and
participate in, which you'll see NijiGo
doing through a series of collaborations
inside Family Mart. Can you develop your
own specific products and lines that
give people an interesting reason to
have demand or want to get something and
pick something up because you know they
have a superior item. And so if I was in
any of those businesses, those more
traditional businesses here in the US,
I'd be thinking about that, right?
Grocery, convenience, what is that
creative director path to enable
product, to enable distribution and
content and to enable experience in a
world where basically everything else
including your proximity and
availability becomes commoditized. And
one interesting thing that I feel ties
really well into marketing and brand
building in general is the next level of
experience in Japanese shopping, which
is the department store. So when you
think of department store in the US, you
think of Macy's, Nordstrom, Saks, and
pretty antiquated models. They feel
nothing like they used to, even Neiman
Marcus the high end. In the UK you have
Harrods and Selfridges and more of a
culture that's built around that. So
they've been, you know, kept some of
that experience at a higher degree of
quality than the US. Whereas in Japan
it's still a primary mode of
consumption. You're still going to a
department store versus individual store
for lots of experiences. And some of the
best brands themselves function both as
a brand and as a department store. The
most interesting one to me is Beams. So
if you hadn't heard of Beams, the story
basically starts with Japan's
reproduction of American garments. So,
there's an obsession in Japanese design
with the heyday of Western craft. The
most authentic American garments of
denim, some of the process that were
used for high-end American workwear are
now really only continued in Japan at
that level of quality. There are brands,
Beams being one of them, that have made
entire businesses off of making the
highest quality versions of kind of
classic American garments. There's a
book on this, Amatora: How Japan Saved
American Style, that you could dive
into. But, American traditional is a
style in Japan that's basically based on
Ivy League style of the US. And what
started as imitation basically became
far more elevated over time. Rather than
importing stuff from the US, Japanese
companies like Beams basically did a
much better version of it locally and
took the craft more seriously. And you
see if you've seen my video on Ralph
Lauren and Kapital, it's another example
of someone who basically took what Ralph
Lauren was doing, but didn't just say,
"Hey, we're going to replicate this."
They made it better and more interesting
and their own over time, which is, for a
lot of brand builders, how to think
about it. I don't want to just make
another T-shirt, I'll make another pair
of pants. How do I elevate the craft?
How do I elevate the story? And so, as
Beams began to develop these originals
at a higher quality standard, they had a
store that would sell imported American
goods. They would curate. They sold
Japan's first Nike trainers and
eventually developed into their own
lines. And this brand blossomed that's
now collaborated with a lot of the
American brands used to base its
products on or be inspired by or carry,
Red Wing, Timberland, Levi's, LL Bean.
And they've created these multi-floor
experiences where you go there and on
the bottom floor of the one I went to,
there was a Ziploc bag collab with
Coquette bows on them. It was like hot
and in demand and people were there to
shop. It was like a standout
collaboration I hadn't seen. It would
you know, rival any collaboration you've
seen in America. With an iconic brand as
a Ziploc bag is. Kind of an outrageous
collab, but one nonetheless and they did
a good job with it. But then there's
curated goods and levels of product,
right? Where they have a lot of products
they're selling of local Japanese and
American, everything from ceramics to
food products to watches. And then it
kind of goes layer by layer through the
store where you have streetwear and
curation around that and the things
you'd expect and toys and vinyl all the
way up to more traditional menswear to
home goods. And it's all looked on
through this lens of Americana, but also
being really true to also finding its
own through lines into Japanese culture
and curation and the merging of the two.
And I think as we see everything in our
world and so much product so come out of
ties where everyone will have a brand
that looks like anyone else's brand.
Everyone makes elevated streetwear.
People who are really passionate about
the same two things, three really, will
find their way to stand out, right? So
first is the craft saying, "Hey, we're
not going to make a crew neck. We're
going to make the best crew neck." And
here's why. "We're going to use
alternative materials." That's why you
see it all the things that I'm so
obsessed with texture. You know when you
get something from me, it's going to be
similar to I've never seen before, but
the texture is going to be unique. And
then there's this idea of history and
the portrayal and where can you spin
from and how do you combine multiple
backgrounds? Yeah, I'm going to take an
Ivy League style with a medieval twist
or a Kapital's Americana mixed with
almost a rural Japanese style. And then
lastly is that experience. How do people
actually engage with this? Cuz doing it
like online and in e-commerce versus
having a world is interesting. That
world doesn't have to just be your own
things you sell. I found when I visited
the UK, a lot of these brands, Earl of
East, had their own products and also
curated others. You bring into an
experience of the brand's world. And in
a world where everything is so ephemeral
and then a new store pops up online
overnight, there's something interesting
about that permanence. You can take a
lot of inspiration from from brands like
Beams. What's also interesting to see
how a brand can be interpreted in a
country. So I'm wearing stuff that I
bought at North Face Japan, North Face
Purple Label, which is an interesting
story. There is a distributor called
Goldwin and they're also a brand, maybe
familiar with them, who used to
distribute North Face. So for those who
don't know, in the '90s North Face was
having a rough time and they almost
faced bankruptcy. They were cooking the
books and a series of deals that they
did, this being one of them, helped kind
of save the company from bankruptcy at
that point. And one of them was they
sold the complete trademark rights to
the name to Goldwin. Not just like a
license that they have or they pay, but
like perpetual ownership of the brand in
Korea and Japan. Goldwin was really
interesting steward of this. They spun
off a sub-brand, I don't want to butcher
the name, where they had really
excellent takes at design and other
branding underneath it. And now
basically 95% of the North Face products
sold in Japan are unique to that market,
and they are excellent. They have
basically a dozen sub-labels. They have
North Face Ultra, they have Purple Label
that I got. There's White Label in South
Korea. There's Standard, there's Sphere.
They have a 141 Custom Service that will
3D scan you to make perfect things to
your body. Really taking the ethos of
The North Face brand and doing what that
brand basically has insisted it won't
do. I poked fun the other year at North
Face launching this new manifesto the
other year that was like AI-written and
completely out of touch with their
customer versus their kind of
traditional tagline that Never Stop
Exploring means a lot to people. But
it's interesting to see in a vacuum as
someone who takes the craft and the
presentation and the elevation of a
brand seriously versus what happens in a
company kind of lumbers on under their
huge holdco ownership and ends up in
like weird corporate stewardship where
that line goes. And I think what's been
done with The North Face in Japan is a
tremendous example of the more
uninhibited creative vision that can
come forth. So much so that I spent a
awful lot of money on clothes there. And
North Face did 1.7 billion in the US
revenue, but 670 million in Japan. It's
a huge business. They coexist. And
there's other stories like this, like
oddball stories of brand ownership. But
Weiser doesn't own its own name in
Germany. Brothers who in like a faux
family feud split up the Aldi grocery
chain in the UK, and then one of those
brothers owns Trader Joe's in the US. So
you have all these little interesting
scenarios that come up around that. I
always just find it interesting to to
look at and think about. And those are
the kind of things that intrigue me a

positioned themselves of being the

That's the entire philosophy of the

It's probably going to be mid. And it's

underneath that branding. And what I

now. Like who's going to sell you what

## 108. When to launch your brand (AVOID these common timing mistakes)

how long does it actually take to
concept prepare sample products and
start your brand it is longer than a lot
of people think but it's also one of
those things where if you start now
whether you're fully invested in your
brand Journey or still working on it or
looking for the future you can actually
knock quite a lot out in part-time and
in chunks until you actually get to
bringing your vision to life in this
video we're going to walk through that
entire journey through what you do when
you're first starting out through your
actual sampling process through
production and the time that that takes
to getting ready for launch and I'm
going to break down as much as I can
inside there so you can have all the

## 109. Why Everything is a “Luxury” Now (brand playbook)

So, in the past few years, we've had
what I call little treat culture. This
is where people have chosen to spend
their money on fancy snacks, fancy
beverages, little purchases, $5, $20 to
basically get that little dopamine hit,
make them feel better about their lives.
This is a cultural trend. But right now,
we're seeing a shift into what I like to
call little luxuries. You've seen this
term likely online. But this is the idea
that you can purchase an everyday item
that is elevated. You basically overpay
for it, pay a premium, but it is
something that is special and appeals to
you. You really care about that
particular part of your day. This could
range anything from like a little shower
head that gives you an amazing
experience with your water to like a
sleep cover to something fun for your
home. Basically, this idea that a better
user experience and a more premium
aesthetic justifies a more premium price
point and why people choose to buy like
that. So, in this video, I'm going to
talk about how we got here. How did this
become such a dominant trend and how to
think about this as a person if you're
actually trying to decide if you want to
buy one and then show you the playbook
if you're developing a brand, how you
participate in this little luxury
culture. Now, I'm going to break down a
dozen brands in the category from tool
kits to laundry detergent sheets to
vegetables. I actually can't wait cuz
these are some of the funnest best
brands to talk about that you are going
to learn from and then you might
actually want to buy. And you can sign
up for a recap of this where I'll
actually list out all those brands and
this playbook in next week's hyper that
is in the description. If you ever want
something like my videos all written
out, I'm going to start providing that
in the newsletter each week. But we're
going to kick off with how we got here.
Why did we get to this little luxury
culture? Let's lock in. So, first let's
talk about the cheapification. I don't
even know if that's a word of
everything. And this comes down to
Amazon and availability of the global
economy. Not to get macroeconomic here,
but there was a massive sea change in
our entire world when Alibaba was
invented. We all know the term Alibaba,
but we aren't super ingrained into what
happened. Really, what happened there is
a platform was created that opened up
factories across the world to the United
States and to entrepreneurs in the
United States. Prior to that, you have
to actually go to China and hunt down a
factory, which is significantly harder
than going online and browsing materials
and communicating through chat. And the
cost savings of this was so massive. You
saw all these new entrepreneurial
businesses pop up. And at the same time,
we had Amazon where anything could get
delivered to your door. And so we saw
people saying, "Hey, I can launch a
brand and just by availability on
Amazon, I can be a choice." Amazon took
off. Everyone wanted to start a business
on there. And this applied to people
that were arbitrageing goods from China
and applied to Chinese brands too where
people said, "We can by virtue of cost
put something online." And so much of
the population buys so many of their
things from Amazon and so much is a
function of price. It led to the overall
quality and then the cost of brands just
continuing to decrease and decrease. It
was all about who could offer something
10 cents cheaper. How can you bundle
more packs inside of it, it became a
genuine race to the bottom. And then
with the shipping scenario, the last few
kind of pre-tariff worlds and Timu and
Shien, all of a sudden they said, "Hey,
we can actually ship goods direct from
China to your door. we can offer even
greater savings on so many things versus
importing cartons and warehousing. And
this has led to basically everything we
consume online a huge chance that there
is kind of like a trash really cheap
item because of the culture associated
with this and these big macroeconomic
shifts. I'm going to take my
macroeconomic hat off real quick and
then we're going to dive into the
entrepreneur hat. So what does that mean
for you? It used to mean if you want to
enter a category as a new brand, one of
the best positions you could take was to
go cheaper. Basically say, "Hey, this
hammer is 20 bucks. I'm going to get it
from China. I'm a smaller business. I
can actually sell that at 18 bucks and I
can get that customer who wants it to be
cheaper. Now that cheap trying to sell
something at a lower cost that is done.
You cannot win in basically any category
on price. It is a horrible method with
which to compete. Instead, what we're
seeing is the idea that hey, well, the
hardest thing to replicate is actual
user experience, aesthetics, content,
all the things that I talk about on this
channel. So, the idea is to make it as
good as you can. Take an item and make
it amazing and then charge a premium for
it. And now there's an erosion of the
middle ground. on the middle ground all
went to the bottom and then all the
people that provide a premier user
experience at the top. And so the brands
that really win kind of live in that
middle to high-end where they get that
premium experience but don't charge an
overweight for it because they've been
able to achieve some scale. This is a
sweet spot for success in the west right
now with a brand versus just with a
product sold on price. And all of these
brands I'm going to show here today are
examples of that. So what is the recipe?
There's a recipe for these products that
will help make them successful. The
first is experience. basically offering
a proumer level product and education
about why it's so good. So let's look at
the coffee machine market for instance.
So you now see these super automatic
machines like the Terra Cafe, you see
Jura, you see Fellow, a bunch of these
companies that came about saying we have
better looking machines. They're easier
to use. They p perform at a proumer
level and they aren't presented or
marketed like the previous generation of
brands and they differentiate purely
through that kind of better super
automatic experience. The second level
is performance. basically quality and
durability. It's doing better than what
you did prior. It filters your water
better. You have a candle that burns
longer. Whatever it is is saying we are
going to have better performance than in
the existing tier of the market. And
these two things go together. And with a
third thing that is mandatory and all
this, that is aesthetic. It's basically
performative, but this idea of the
aesthetics of everything. How can you
make everything in your home or
everything you choose to interact with
more beautiful? One of the items I'm
going to walk through here today is
called the Tinker Toolkit. Is literally
a tool kit that has been like
millennialified. It does have some
experience pluses and the grips and the
way it's put together, but literally
it's just a more beautiful looking
nature of an existing thing in your
home. And there are hundreds of examples
of products like that. And the three of
these together, experience, performance,
and aesthetic, are the holy grail of
being able to market an item that you
can charge a premium for. And one of the
reasons for that is if you have those
three things, they is built for standout
content. You're able to constantly
explain in ads why it's better because
of the experience you provide, because
of the performance it delivers. You have
actual stats you can rattle off and say
this is why this is worth more money.
And then you have the nature of hey,
this is a beautiful looking thing. So it
immediately hooks better and looks
better in content, which is an innate
advantage. Take a little break for a
personal anecdote about how I learned
this. So I used to uh be a partner in an
agency called Gwyn Partners when I lived
in Texas. We were a product development
agency and we did some go to market as
well. I was working on this with an
entrepreneur friend of mine named Colin
Gwyn who is a person who has been the
most obsessed with user experience in
hardware product and product overall of
anyone I've ever met. He is lives in the
religion of product experience
development. It's what he thinks about
how he thinks the way I can ideulate and
think about content and give you an idea
or a strategy in seconds. He thinks that
way about product. And one of the things
that we worked on there in addition to
applying that methodology to a lot of
products and he'd worked on things like
the DJI Phantom when it initially came
out as one of his projects. So was a DJI
Inspire. He's also the person that came
up with the idea for Gel Blaster and how
it was packaged in the United States.
Many people know I worked there too. It
actually spun out of that agency. But we
developed Gel Blaster because he saw
that vision in the internal hours of the
company. So we would dedicate a certain
percentage of hours at our agency to our
internal projects. There's a couple
interesting ones of those. The current

## 110. Why every product sucks now (enshittication)

Does it seem like everything we buy kind
of sucks now? The portions are smaller,
the average price of basic things is
ridiculous, and the quality of things we
used to like is significantly worse.
Brands that used to stand for something
are the same as everyone else, and it's
hard to actually navigate the web of
lies about what's made where, what
quality, and what's going on. This is
the age of enshittification, and you are
not alone. It is happening to all of us.
In this video, we're going to talk about
why. We're going to talk about the lies
of what's made where around the world.
We're going to talk about the quality of
cashmere and leather. We're going to
talk about the education of the consumer
and how that will helps and hurts the
scenario. We're going to talk about
everyone starting brands and what that
has actually meant for the products we
consume, mass production, internet
advertising, what you can learn to be
educated both as a consumer and if
you're someone that partakes in this
economy as a marketer or a brand owner.
I am fascinated by this topic, the
culture of dupes, design, who owns what
ideas, who makes what where, and what
this means for our collective taste or
lack thereof. I'm excited for y'all to
explore with me. Let's lock in. So,
first, I want to start with this idea of
provenance. We used to be able to know
what we'd get from buying something a
lot based on where it was made. Used to
assume if something came from China that
it was some sort of kind of crappy
mass-produced good, and by contrast, if
it was made in Italy that it would be
something amazing. But along the line,
we began to get sold lies. So, let's
start with the luxury brands and the
lies that they've been telling. So, a
few years back, outside of Milan,
Italian authorities raided a Dior
factory, and they found basically
terrible conditions. Migrants were
living there, safety guards have been
taken off against the machines, and
people were working far against Italian
labor laws who were not people from
Italy to produce goods that are made in
Italy. Similar things have happened to
Valentino, to Armani. You can read about
the charges for these people online. In
some cases, it may be, "Oh, this is a
rogue member of our supply chain." If
it's an Italian factory coming from an
Italian company, it's only so rogue. At
some points it's actually part of that
company's infrastructure. And it
highlights this idea of made in Italy as
luxury. So there's the concept of
something made in a country, and there's
the concept of how and who makes it. So
for instance, when you are bringing in
migrants from countries like China or
Vietnam or Burma who make those things
for the rest of the world, and you're
simply having them do business inside
your factory, and then removing all of
the safety guards for what is allowable
by regulation to work, and that good is
still legally made in Italy, are you
buying that artisan experience that gets
suggested? This is what the luxury

## 112. Why some creators make way more than others (content niches ranked)

So, this last week I was in London
speaking at Vervant Pulse to hundreds of
brands about how to use creators. And it
got me thinking about the creator
economy and how misunderstood it is. I
want to make a video to discuss how are
there so many creators and then how are
they all making money? And this is a
particularly poignant subject because I
met with a lot of brands in the UK and
they're still really focused there on
old school brand strategy. Less have
kind of come over to the new modern
marketing than we see in the US. And it
was just a reminder of how early we
still are on the bell curve of this. In
this video, I'm going to break down all
of the ways that creators make money.
Affiliate, influence, UGC, working for
brands, info products, payouts. I'm
going to talk about the best niches for
creators to make money and I'm going to
rank them because not all niches are
created equal. And then I'm going to go
into how brands are leveraging creators
and how they can all those different
ways so both brands can understand how
they take advantage of this and creators
can also understand how brands actually
want to pay them and how it works. It'll
be a complete A to Z of the creator
economy. If you want to leverage
influence online to make money, I'm
going to drop an enormous amount of info
on you right here. Let's stroll through
sunny London and lock it.
So, first, why do we even care? Who
cares about all these creators, all
these influencers? Well, 82% of
individuals 18 to 44 have posted content
online in the last month. 64% of Gen Z
consider themselves to be content
creators. It is a different world.
However many creators you think there
are, there are more. And however much
money you think they're making, it's
more. So, let's start with how creators
